1:
"And it came to pass, that, while Apollos was at Corinth, Paul having passed through the upper coasts came to Ephesus: and finding certain disciples," The witnesses note Paul's arrival at Ephesus and the finding of certain disciples. Matthew Henry notes Ephesus was a city of great note in Asia, famous for the temple of Diana (one of the wonders of the world); Paul came to plant there while Apollos watered at Corinth, and "grudged not that Apollos entered into his labours... but rejoiced in it." Gill notes Apollos was at Corinth, where he watered what Paul had planted. The witnesses note Paul, coming to Ephesus, finding certain disciples — believers, yet (as appears) imperfectly instructed; the providence bringing Paul to them. The witnesses note the arrival at Ephesus. The witnesses note the disciples found.

The witnesses note Paul coming to Ephesus and finding certain disciples — believers, yet imperfectly instructed.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's arrival at Ephesus and the finding of the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence bringing Paul to the imperfectly instructed disciples, and the harmony of the labourers. The witnesses note the city: Ephesus, a city of great note in Asia, famous for the temple of Diana (Matthew Henry); a great center, where Paul would labour long and fruitfully. The expositors observe the harmony of the labourers that Henry marks: while Apollos watered at Corinth, Paul planted at Ephesus; and Paul grudged not that Apollos entered into his labours and built on his foundation, but rejoiced; the fellow-workers labouring in harmony, without rivalry; "I have planted, Apollos watered; but God gave the increase" (1 Corinthians 3:6). The witnesses note the finding of the disciples: "finding certain disciples" — Paul found at Ephesus certain disciples, believers; yet (as the next verses show) imperfectly instructed, having only the baptism of John, not having heard of the Pentecostal gift of the Spirit; disciples in some sense, but lacking full instruction. The expositors draw the lesson on the imperfectly instructed: these disciples had received what they had heard — John's baptism, the repentance and the hope of the Messiah — but had not heard the fuller gospel; sincere, yet imperfect; needing further teaching; the providence bringing Paul to instruct them. The witnesses observe the providence in the meeting: Paul, coming to Ephesus, found these disciples — that he might instruct them more perfectly, baptize them into Christ, and bring them into the fulness of the gospel; the providence supplying the teacher for the imperfectly taught. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's arrival at Ephesus, the harmony of the labourers, and the finding of the imperfectly instructed disciples. The verse thus records Paul coming to Ephesus and finding certain disciples — believers imperfectly instructed — teaching the providence bringing the teacher to them and the harmony of the labourers in the Lord's work.
2:
"He said unto them, Have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed? And they said unto him, We have not so much as heard whether there be any Holy Ghost." The witnesses note Paul's question and the disciples' ignorance of the Spirit's gift. Barnes clarifies the question: "Have ye received the extraordinary effusions and miraculous influences of the Holy Ghost?" — not the ordinary converting grace (which Paul would not doubt they had, having believed), but the miraculous gifts conferred on most societies of believers. Gill agrees the reference is to the extraordinary gifts. On the disciples' answer, the meaning is that they had not heard whether the Holy Ghost was yet given (the Pentecostal outpouring), not that they were ignorant of the Spirit's existence. The witnesses note Paul asking whether they had received the Spirit's gift, and their not having heard of the outpouring. The witnesses note the question of the Spirit. The witnesses note the disciples' ignorance.

The witnesses note Paul's question on the Spirit's gift — and the disciples' not having heard whether the Holy Ghost was given.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's question and the disciples' ignorance of the Spirit's gift. The lesson the expositors draw is the distinction between the ordinary grace and the extraordinary gift, and the disciples' want of fuller instruction. The witnesses note Paul's question: "Have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed?" — Barnes and Gill clarify that Paul asks not of the ordinary, converting grace of the Spirit (which he would not doubt they had, having believed — for the renewing of the heart is the Spirit's work), but of the extraordinary, miraculous gifts of the Spirit (tongues, prophecy), which were conferred on most societies of believers in that age; the question of the extraordinary endowment. The expositors observe the distinction: the ordinary grace (regeneration, faith) is given to all believers; the extraordinary gifts (the miraculous endowments) were a special, visible gift of the apostolic age; Paul asks whether they had received the latter. The witnesses note the disciples' answer: "We have not so much as heard whether there be any Holy Ghost" — the meaning, the expositors hold, is not that they were ignorant of the Spirit's existence (for as disciples of John they would know of the Holy Ghost from the Scriptures), but that they had not heard whether the Holy Ghost was yet given — the Pentecostal outpouring, the gift of the Spirit; they had not heard of the great event; "the Holy Ghost was not yet given" (John 7:39). The expositors draw the lesson on the want of instruction: these disciples, having only John's baptism, knew the repentance and the hope, but had not heard of the gift of the Spirit poured out; imperfectly instructed, ignorant of the fuller gospel; needing Paul's teaching. The witnesses observe the gentleness of the inquiry: Paul, finding them disciples, inquires of their state, that he might instruct them; the wise teacher drawing out their want, to supply it; the question opening the way for fuller teaching. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's question on the Spirit's gift, the distinction of ordinary and extraordinary, and the disciples' want of instruction. The verse thus records Paul's question and the disciples' ignorance — not having heard whether the Holy Ghost was given — teaching the distinction between the ordinary grace and the extraordinary gift, and the disciples' need of fuller instruction.
3:
"And he said unto them, Unto what then were ye baptized? And they said, Unto John's baptism." The witnesses note Paul's inquiry into their baptism. Barnes explains the question — "Unto what faith, or doctrine? What did you profess to believe when you were baptized?" — and the answer, that they were baptized "unto John's baptism," receiving John's baptism only. Gill notes Paul takes it for granted they were baptized (being disciples), and asks into what; the answer showing they had received only the baptism of John. The witnesses note the disciples confessing they had received John's baptism — the baptism of repentance, pointing to Christ, but not the fuller Christian baptism nor the gift of the Spirit. The witnesses note John's baptism. The witnesses note the inquiry into baptism.

The witnesses note the disciples confessing they had received only John's baptism — the baptism of repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse the inquiry into their baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the imperfection of the disciples' instruction, shown in their having received only John's baptism. The witnesses note Paul's question: "Unto what then were ye baptized?" — Barnes explains the sense: unto what faith or doctrine were you baptized? what did you profess when baptized? Gill notes Paul takes for granted they were baptized (being disciples), and asks into what; the inquiry into the meaning and content of their baptism. The expositors observe the answer: "Unto John's baptism" — the disciples confessed they had received the baptism of John; the baptism of repentance, administered by John, pointing forward to the Messiah; they had received John's baptism, but not the fuller Christian baptism nor the gift of the Spirit. The witnesses press the imperfection this revealed: the disciples had received John's baptism — the repentance, the hope of the coming Christ — but had not been instructed in the fulness of the gospel; their baptism preparatory, pointing forward, but not the complete Christian initiation; the want of fuller teaching. The expositors draw the lesson on the place of John's baptism: it was the baptism of repentance, preparing the way for Christ, pointing to him as the coming One; a true and divine baptism, yet preparatory; those who had received it needing further instruction in the gospel of the risen, ascended Christ and the gift of the Spirit. The witnesses observe the wisdom of Paul's inquiry: by asking into their baptism, Paul discovered the root of their imperfection — they had only John's baptism; and so could instruct them rightly, leading them from John to Christ; the wise teacher tracing the want to its source. The expositors note the disciples' honesty: they plainly confessed what baptism they had received; the teachable, honest spirit; ready to learn; the disciples open to instruction. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the inquiry into their baptism, the imperfection of John's baptism only, and the want of fuller instruction. The verse thus records the disciples confessing John's baptism — the baptism of repentance only — teaching the imperfection of their instruction and the need to be led from John to Christ.
4:
"Then said Paul, John verily baptized with the baptism of repentance, saying unto the people, that they should believe on him which should come after him, that is, on Christ Jesus." The witnesses note Paul's explanation of John's baptism. Barnes explains: John baptized with the baptism of repentance (a profession of repentance), and the design of his coming was to turn the people from sin and prepare them for the Messiah, directing their attention to him that was to come (John 1:15, 22-27). Gill notes Paul shows John's baptism agreeable to, and the same as, the baptism of Christ, pointing to him. The witnesses note Paul instructing the disciples that John's baptism was the baptism of repentance, pointing them to believe on Christ who came after — leading them from John to Christ. The witnesses note John's baptism explained. The witnesses note pointing to Christ.

The witnesses note Paul explaining John's baptism — the baptism of repentance pointing to Christ, who came after John.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's explanation of John's baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is that John's baptism pointed to Christ, and Paul leads the disciples from John to Christ. The witnesses note the explanation: "John verily baptized with the baptism of repentance" — Paul explains that John's baptism was the baptism of repentance; Barnes notes it had special relation to repentance, a profession that they repented of their sins; the baptism that called men to turn from sin. The expositors observe the design of John's baptism: "saying unto the people, that they should believe on him which should come after him, that is, on Christ Jesus" — John's purpose was to direct the people to the coming Messiah; he baptized them to repentance, and pointed them to believe on Christ who came after him (John 1:15, 22-27); John's baptism preparatory, pointing forward to Christ. The witnesses press Paul's leading from John to Christ: Paul shows the disciples that John himself pointed to Christ; that the baptism of repentance was to prepare them to believe on the Messiah; that John's ministry culminated in Christ; the apostle leading them from the preparatory to the fulfilment, from John to Jesus. The expositors draw the lesson on the place of John: John was the forerunner, his baptism preparatory, his message pointing to Christ; the disciples who had John's baptism were to be led to Christ, the One John foretold; John not the end but the way to Christ. The witnesses observe the harmony of John's baptism with Christ's: Gill notes Paul shows John's baptism agreeable to, and pointing to, Christ; not opposed to the gospel, but preparatory; John and Christ in harmony, the forerunner and the Lord; the baptism of repentance leading to faith in Christ. The expositors note the wisdom of Paul's instruction: he does not disparage John, but shows that John pointed to Christ; leading the disciples gently from what they knew (John) to what they needed (Christ); the wise teacher building on what they had. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's explanation of John's baptism, its pointing to Christ, and the leading of the disciples from John to Jesus. The verse thus records Paul's explanation of John's baptism — the baptism of repentance pointing to Christ — teaching that John's baptism led to Christ, and Paul leads the disciples from the forerunner to the Lord.
5:
"When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus." The witnesses note the baptism of the disciples in Christ's name, and the question of rebaptism. Barnes addresses the question whether these were rebaptized: some critics, supposing rebaptism never done, take this verse as Luke's narrative of John's converts; "but this is a most forced construction; and it is evident" that the disciples, hearing Paul, were now baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. Gill agrees they were baptized, now in Christ's name. The witnesses note the disciples, having heard Paul, baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus — receiving Christian baptism, distinct from the baptism of John they had before. The witnesses note the baptism in Christ's name. The witnesses note the rebaptism question.

The witnesses note the disciples baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus — receiving Christian baptism after John's.

So the witnesses make the verse the baptism of the disciples in Christ's name. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciples, having only John's baptism, were now baptized into Christ — the rebaptism debated but, on the natural reading, affirmed. The witnesses note the baptism: "when they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus" — the disciples, having heard Paul's instruction (that John pointed to Christ), were now baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus; receiving Christian baptism, into Christ. The expositors address the question of rebaptism that Barnes weighs: some critics, holding that rebaptism was never practiced, take this verse not as recording the disciples' baptism by Paul, but as Luke's narrative of John's converts (that John told them to believe on Christ, and baptized them); but Barnes calls this a most forced construction; the natural reading is that the disciples, hearing Paul, were now baptized in Christ's name — having had only John's baptism, they now received Christian baptism. The witnesses press the distinction: John's baptism (which they had) was preparatory, pointing to the coming Christ; Christian baptism, in the name of the Lord Jesus, is into the accomplished work of Christ, the risen and ascended Lord; the disciples, having the former, now received the latter; the imperfect baptism completed by the full. The expositors draw the lesson, with the qualification: this is not a precedent for the general rebaptism of those validly baptized as Christians; for these had only John's baptism, not Christian baptism; the case peculiar — those baptized into John, before the gospel was fully come, now baptized into Christ; not the rebaptism of Christians, but the completing of those who had only the forerunner's baptism. The witnesses observe the difference of John's and Christian baptism in this case: John's baptism, given before the full revelation, was preparatory; Christian baptism, into the name of the Lord Jesus (and, the full form, the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost), is the complete initiation; these disciples, lacking the latter, received it. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the disciples baptized into Christ, the rebaptism debated, and the completing of John's baptism by Christian baptism. The verse thus records the disciples baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus — receiving Christian baptism after John's — teaching the completing of the imperfectly baptized, the rebaptism affirmed on the natural reading, yet peculiar to those who had only John's baptism.
6:
"And when Paul had laid his hands upon them, the Holy Ghost came on them; and they spake with tongues, and prophesied." The witnesses note the conferring of the extraordinary gift by Paul's hands. Gill notes they had been baptized (by John, and now in Christ's name), and Paul laid hands on them, as Peter and John on the Samaritans; and the same extraordinary effects followed. Clarke notes they received the miraculous gift of different languages, in which they taught the doctrines of the Christian religion. The witnesses note Paul laying hands on the disciples, and the Holy Ghost coming on them with the extraordinary gifts — speaking with tongues and prophesying; the apostolic conferring of the gift. The witnesses note the gift conferred. The witnesses note tongues and prophecy.

The witnesses note the Spirit's gift conferred by Paul's hands — the disciples speaking with tongues and prophesying.

So the witnesses make the verse the conferring of the extraordinary gift. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic conferring of the Spirit's gift, the disciples receiving the extraordinary endowments. The witnesses note the act: "when Paul had laid his hands upon them, the Holy Ghost came on them" — Paul, after they were baptized into Christ, laid his hands on them, and the Holy Ghost came on them; Gill compares Peter and John laying hands on the Samaritans (Acts 8:17), with the same effect; the apostolic conferring of the gift. The expositors observe the gift: "they spake with tongues, and prophesied" — the disciples received the extraordinary gifts of the Spirit; Clarke notes they received the miraculous gift of different languages, in which they taught the doctrines of the Christian religion; the visible, extraordinary endowments — tongues and prophecy. The witnesses press the nature of the gift: this was the extraordinary gift Paul had asked of (v.2) — the miraculous endowments of the apostolic age, tongues and prophecy; not the ordinary regenerating grace (which they had as believers), but the special, visible gifts; the gift conferred by the apostle's hands. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostolic means: the extraordinary gift came through the laying on of the apostle's hands; the apostolic prerogative (as at Samaria, Acts 8); the means by which the extraordinary gifts were conferred in that age; the apostle the instrument, God the giver. The witnesses observe the completion of the disciples' instruction: imperfectly instructed (v.2-3), they were now baptized into Christ (v.5), and received the gift of the Spirit (v.6); brought from the preparatory baptism of John to the fulness of the gospel and the Spirit's gift; their instruction completed. The expositors note the careful reading, against making this a universal pattern: the extraordinary gifts (tongues, prophecy) and the apostolic conferring belonged to the apostolic age; not a pattern for an ordinary "second blessing," but the conferring of the special gifts of that time; the extraordinary endowments peculiar to the founding of the church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gift conferred by Paul's hands, the disciples speaking with tongues and prophesying, and the completion of their instruction. The verse thus records the conferring of the extraordinary gift — the Spirit coming on them, tongues and prophecy — teaching the apostolic conferring of the Spirit's gift and the completion of the disciples' instruction.
7:
"And all the men were about twelve." The witnesses note the small number of the disciples. Gill notes the number was small, so the historian might be certain of it; and that these "seem to be the first materials of a Gospel church at Ephesus, which afterwards was very large and flourishing," and very likely some became elders of it. Ellicott suggests they were living together as a kind of ascetic community, attending the meetings of the Church yet not sharing the fulness of its life. The witnesses note the disciples about twelve in number — a small beginning, the first materials of the great Ephesian church. The witnesses note the small number. The witnesses note the beginning of the church.

The witnesses note the twelve disciples — a small beginning, the first materials of the great Ephesian church.

So the witnesses make the verse the small number of the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the small beginning of the great Ephesian church, from these twelve disciples. The witnesses note the number: "all the men were about twelve" — Gill notes the number was small, so the historian could be certain of it; about twelve men, a small company. The expositors observe Gill's note on their significance: these seem to be the first materials of a gospel church at Ephesus, which afterwards became very large and flourishing; and very likely some of these twelve became elders of it; the small beginning of a great church. The witnesses press the smallness of the beginning: about twelve disciples — a tiny company; yet from this small beginning grew the great Ephesian church, to which Paul wrote his epistle, where he laboured long, and which became a leading church; the great work from the small start; the mustard seed. The expositors draw the lesson on the small beginnings of great works: God begins great things from small; the Ephesian church from twelve disciples; the believer not to despise the day of small things (Zechariah 4:10); the great harvest from the small field. The witnesses observe Ellicott's suggestion about their state: these disciples seem to have been living together as a kind of ascetic community, attending the meetings of the Church yet not sharing the fulness of its life; a separate, imperfectly instructed group, now brought into the fulness; the providence bringing them in. The expositors note the providence in the finding and completing of these twelve: Paul found them, instructed them, baptized them into Christ, and conferred the gift; the twelve brought into the fulness of the gospel; the first materials of the church established. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the small number of the disciples, the great Ephesian church from this small beginning, and the providence completing the twelve. The verse thus records the small number of the disciples — about twelve — teaching the small beginning of the great Ephesian church and the providence bringing the twelve into the fulness.
8:
"And he went into the synagogue, and spake boldly for the space of three months, disputing and persuading the things concerning the kingdom of God." The witnesses note Paul's bold preaching in the synagogue. Chrysostom notes Paul "in every place forcing his way into the synagogue," wishing the occasion to be given him by the Jews themselves; "for three months arguing and persuading concerning the kingdom of God"; provoking them to jealousy, that the Gentiles receiving the word, the Jews might repent. The witnesses note Paul, for three months, preaching boldly in the synagogue, disputing and persuading the things of the kingdom of God — the bold, reasoned, persuasive ministry to the Jews first. The witnesses note the bold preaching. The witnesses note the kingdom of God.

The witnesses note Paul preaching boldly in the synagogue for three months — disputing and persuading concerning the kingdom of God.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's bold preaching in the synagogue. The lesson the expositors draw is the bold, reasoned, persuasive ministry of Paul, beginning with the Jews. The witnesses note the boldness: "he went into the synagogue, and spake boldly for the space of three months" — Paul, according to his custom, went first to the synagogue, and preached boldly for three months; the courage of the apostle, openly proclaiming the gospel to the Jews; the bold, fearless preaching. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on Paul's method: he forced his way into the synagogue in every place, wishing the occasion (for turning to the Gentiles) to be given by the Jews themselves; preaching boldly, yet with argument and persuasion; provoking the Jews to jealousy, that the Gentiles receiving the word, the Jews might repent; the wise strategy of beginning with the Jews. The witnesses press the manner of his preaching: "disputing and persuading the things concerning the kingdom of God" — Paul disputed (reasoned, argued from the Scriptures) and persuaded (urged, applied to the heart) the things of the kingdom of God; not mere assertion, but reasoned argument and earnest persuasion; the kingdom of God his theme. The expositors draw the lesson on the reasoned, persuasive ministry: Paul's preaching was bold, but also reasoned (disputing) and persuasive (persuading); the apostle argued from the Scriptures and pressed the truth on the conscience; the ministry that both convinces the mind and moves the heart; "knowing therefore the terror of the Lord, we persuade men" (2 Corinthians 5:11). The witnesses observe the subject: "the kingdom of God" — Paul preached the kingdom of God; the reign of God in Christ, the gospel of the kingdom; the great theme of the apostolic preaching; the kingdom proclaimed. The expositors note the duration: three months in the synagogue — a long and patient ministry to the Jews; the apostle giving them full opportunity, preaching boldly and patiently; the Jews given the first and ample offer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's bold preaching, the reasoned and persuasive ministry, and the kingdom of God proclaimed. The verse thus records Paul's bold preaching in the synagogue — disputing and persuading concerning the kingdom — teaching the bold, reasoned, persuasive ministry of the apostle, beginning with the Jews.
9:
"But when divers were hardened, and believed not, but spake evil of that way before the multitude, he departed from them, and separated the disciples, disputing daily in the school of one Tyrannus." The witnesses note the hardening of some and Paul's separating of the disciples. Gill notes that while some were convinced, others were hardened and remained incredulous; "for the Gospel, while it is the savour of life unto life to some, it is the savour of death unto death to others; as the sun melts the wax, and hardens the clay." Barnes notes "hardened" denotes insensibility and opposition to the gospel; they spake evil of "that way" (the gospel). The witnesses note Paul, when some hardened and reviled the gospel, withdrawing the disciples to the school of Tyrannus, teaching daily there. The witnesses note the hardening. The witnesses note the separating of the disciples.

The witnesses note some hardened and reviling the gospel — Paul withdrawing the disciples to the school of Tyrannus.

So the witnesses make the verse the hardening of some and Paul's separating of the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the twofold effect of the gospel, and the wisdom of withdrawing the disciples to a separate place. The witnesses note the hardening: "divers were hardened, and believed not, but spake evil of that way" — while some were convinced, others were hardened, remained unbelieving, and spoke evil of the gospel ("that way") before the multitude; Barnes notes "hardened" denotes insensibility and opposition; the gospel rejected and reviled by some. The expositors observe Gill's lesson on the twofold effect: the gospel is the savour of life unto life to some, the savour of death unto death to others (2 Corinthians 2:16); as the sun melts the wax and hardens the clay, so the same gospel softens the believing and hardens the unbelieving; the difference in the heart, not the message; the gospel a touchstone, dividing men. The witnesses press the reviling: they "spake evil of that way before the multitude" — the hardened not only disbelieved, but reviled the gospel publicly; the opposition turning to blasphemy and public slander; the enemies of the gospel railing against it. The expositors draw the lesson on the hardening effect of the rejected gospel: those who reject the gospel are hardened by it; the truth refused leaves the heart more obdurate; the gospel that softens the believing hardens the rejecters; the savour of death to those who will not believe. The witnesses note Paul's response: "he departed from them, and separated the disciples, disputing daily in the school of one Tyrannus" — Paul, when the synagogue grew hostile, withdrew the disciples and taught daily in the school of Tyrannus (a lecture hall or school); the wisdom of separating the disciples from the hardened, to a place where the gospel could be freely taught; the prudence that withdraws from fruitless opposition to a more open field. The expositors observe the wisdom of the separation: Paul did not contend endlessly with the hardened, but withdrew the disciples to a separate place; the wisdom of leaving the obstinate and gathering the willing; the ministry continued in a more favourable setting; the school of Tyrannus the new base. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the twofold effect of the gospel, the hardening of the rejecters, and the wisdom of withdrawing the disciples. The verse thus records the hardening of some and Paul's separating of the disciples — to the school of Tyrannus — teaching the twofold effect of the gospel and the wisdom of withdrawing from fruitless opposition.
10:
"And this continued by the space of two years; so that all they which dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus, both Jews and Greeks." The witnesses note the two years' teaching and the wide spread of the gospel. Barnes notes the public instruction continued two whole years, so that the great mass of the people in the province of Asia (of which Ephesus was the capital) heard the word; Ephesus being a center of commerce and worship, with a constant influx of people. The witnesses note Paul teaching two years in the school of Tyrannus, so that all Asia — Jews and Greeks — heard the word of the Lord Jesus; the gospel spreading widely from the Ephesian center. The witnesses note the two years. The witnesses note the wide spread.

The witnesses note the two years' teaching — so that all Asia, Jews and Greeks, heard the word of the Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the wide spread of the gospel from Ephesus. The lesson the expositors draw is the fruit of Paul's patient, prolonged ministry, the gospel reaching all Asia. The witnesses note the duration: "this continued by the space of two years" — Paul's public teaching in the school of Tyrannus continued two whole years; the patient, prolonged ministry; the apostle settled at Ephesus, teaching daily for years; the steady, persevering work. The expositors observe the wide spread that Barnes marks: "all they which dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus, both Jews and Greeks" — so that the great mass of the people in the province of Asia (of which Ephesus was the capital) heard the word; Ephesus being a center of commerce and worship, with a constant influx of people, the gospel spread from there throughout the province; the word reaching all Asia. The witnesses press the strategic value of Ephesus: a great center, where people from all Asia came for commerce and the worship of Diana; the gospel preached there reaching, through the visitors and the converts, the whole province; the strategic city the base for the gospel's spread; the center radiating the word. The expositors draw the lesson on the fruit of patient ministry: Paul's two years of daily teaching bore great fruit — all Asia heard the word; the patient, persevering ministry the means of wide spread; the steady work, not the brief visit, filling the province; the fruit of perseverance. The witnesses observe the breadth of the hearing: "both Jews and Greeks" — the word reached both Jews and Gentiles; the gospel embracing all; the whole province, of every kind, hearing; the breadth of the reach. The expositors note the means of the spread: not Paul alone, but the converts and the visitors carrying the word; the gospel spreading from the center through many; the word published throughout Asia (as the churches of Asia, Revelation 2-3, may have begun); the wide harvest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the two years' teaching, the gospel reaching all Asia, and the fruit of patient ministry. The verse thus records the two years' teaching and the wide spread — all Asia hearing the word — teaching the fruit of Paul's patient, prolonged ministry from the Ephesian center.
11:
"And God wrought special miracles by the hands of Paul:" The witnesses note the special miracles God wrought by Paul. Gill notes these were "not any sort of miracles, common and vulgar ones... but they were rare and uncommon ones, and in which there was a visible display of the power of God; to whom as the efficient cause they are ascribed, the apostle being only an instrument." Barnes notes "special miracles" — remarkable, uncommon, very unusual. The witnesses note God working special, extraordinary miracles by the hands of Paul — confirming his doctrine and mission; the miracles God's work, Paul the instrument. The witnesses note the special miracles. The witnesses note God the worker.

The witnesses note God working special miracles by Paul's hands — extraordinary, confirming his ministry, God the worker.

So the witnesses make the verse the special miracles God wrought by Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is the extraordinary nature of the miracles and that God was the worker, Paul the instrument. The witnesses note the miracles: "God wrought special miracles by the hands of Paul" — God worked, by Paul's hands, special, extraordinary miracles; Gill notes these were not common or vulgar miracles, but rare and uncommon ones, with a visible display of God's power; Barnes notes "special" denotes remarkable, uncommon, very unusual. The expositors observe the extraordinariness: the miracles were not ordinary, but special, remarkable — beyond the usual; the handkerchiefs and aprons from Paul's body healing the sick and casting out spirits (v.12); the extraordinary works confirming the apostle's ministry. The witnesses press that God was the worker: "God wrought" — the miracles were God's work, ascribed to him as the efficient cause; Paul only the instrument, the hands through which God worked; the power God's, not the apostle's; the miracles the work of God, the apostle his instrument. The expositors draw the lesson on the source of the miracles: the miracles were God's, not Paul's; "God wrought... by the hands of Paul"; the apostle the channel, God the power; the glory God's; the believer not to ascribe to the instrument what belongs to God. The witnesses observe the purpose of the miracles: to confirm Paul's doctrine and mission; the extraordinary works attesting the gospel he preached and his apostolic authority; the miracles the credentials of the messenger; the signs confirming the word. The expositors note the guard against superstition implied: the miracles were God's special works, not magic or the apostle's own power; against the magic and superstition of Ephesus (v.13-19), the true miracles of God; the contrast of the divine power with the magical arts. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the special miracles, the extraordinary works of God, and God the worker through Paul. The verse thus records the special miracles God wrought by Paul — extraordinary, confirming his ministry — teaching the extraordinary nature of the miracles and that God was the worker, Paul the instrument.
12:
"So that from his body were brought unto the sick handkerchiefs or aprons, and the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out of them." The witnesses note the healing through the handkerchiefs and aprons. Gill notes the handkerchiefs (with which Paul wiped his face) and aprons (which he wore at his trade) were brought from his body to the sick, and used by them. Barnes notes these were cloths applied to his body, which he had used or touched. The witnesses note the extraordinary miracle: cloths from Paul's body, brought to the sick, healing diseases and casting out spirits — God honouring the faith of those who sought him, not the cloth itself; the power God's, the faith the channel. The witnesses note the handkerchiefs. The witnesses note the extraordinary cures.

The witnesses note cloths from Paul's body healing the sick — God honouring faith, not the cloth, the power his.

So the witnesses make the verse the healing through the handkerchiefs and aprons. The lesson the expositors draw is the extraordinary power of God, honouring the faith of those who sought him, not the cloth itself. The witnesses note the means: "from his body were brought unto the sick handkerchiefs or aprons" — Gill notes the handkerchiefs (with which Paul wiped his face) and aprons (which he wore at his trade) were brought from his body to the sick; Barnes notes cloths he had used or touched; the cloths carried to the sick. The expositors observe the cures: "the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out of them" — the sick were healed and the demon-possessed delivered by these cloths; the extraordinary miracle, the power working through the cloths from Paul's body. The witnesses press the careful understanding: the power was not in the cloth itself (as a charm or relic), but in God, who honoured the faith of those who sought him; the cloths the means God used, the faith of the seekers the channel; the power God's, not the cloth's; against superstition, the true power of God. The expositors draw the lesson on God honouring faith: as the woman was healed by touching Christ's garment (Matthew 9:21), and as Peter's shadow (Acts 5:15), so here God honoured the faith of those who sought him through the cloths; the faith, not the material, the channel; God condescending to the faith of the seekers; the power his, the faith the means. The witnesses observe the guard against the abuse of relics: the witnesses are careful to ascribe the power to God, not the cloths; against the later superstition of relics, the true sense — God honouring faith, not the material; the extraordinary miracle not a warrant for relic-worship, but a display of God's power honouring faith. The expositors note the extraordinariness peculiar to the apostolic age: these special miracles (v.11) belonged to the founding of the church, confirming the gospel in a city of magic; not an ordinary pattern, but the extraordinary works of that time; the special signs of the apostolic age. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the healing through the cloths, God honouring faith, and the power ascribed to God, not the material. The verse thus records the healing through the handkerchiefs and aprons — the diseases departing, the spirits cast out — teaching the extraordinary power of God, honouring the faith of the seekers, not the cloth itself.
13:
"Then certain of the vagabond Jews, exorcists, took upon them to call over them which had evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth." The witnesses note the vagabond Jewish exorcists' attempt to use the name of Jesus. Gill describes them as Jews who strolled from place to place, pretending to cure diseases by charms and to dispossess devils by conjuration. Matthew Henry notes the gospel was sent to war against Satan, Christ going forth conquering; here is the conquest of Satan even in those voluntarily devoted to him. The witnesses note the wandering exorcists, seeing the power of the name, attempting to use it themselves — "We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth" — using the name as a charm, without faith; the presumption that would be exposed. The witnesses note the exorcists. The witnesses note the misuse of the name.

The witnesses note the vagabond exorcists trying to use the name of Jesus as a charm — without faith, a presumption soon exposed.

So the witnesses make the verse the exorcists' attempt to use the name of Jesus. The lesson the expositors draw is the presumption of using the name of Jesus as a charm, without faith, soon to be exposed. The witnesses note the exorcists: "certain of the vagabond Jews, exorcists" — Gill describes them as Jews who strolled from place to place, pretending to cure diseases by charms and to cast out devils by conjuration; wandering impostors, dealing in magic and exorcism; the pretended exorcists. The expositors observe their attempt: "took upon them to call over them which had evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth" — seeing the power of the name of Jesus in Paul's hands, the exorcists attempted to use it themselves, adjuring the spirits by Jesus whom Paul preached; using the name as a charm, a magic formula; the presumption of borrowing the name without faith. The witnesses press the misuse of the name: the exorcists used the name of Jesus as a charm, a word of power, without faith in him; "Jesus whom Paul preacheth" — they knew the name only as Paul's, not as their own Lord; the name used superstitiously, without belief; the presumption of the unbelieving. The expositors draw the lesson on using the name without faith: the name of Jesus is mighty, but only to faith; to use it as a charm, without believing in him, is presumption and folly; the name not a magic word, but the power of the Lord to those who believe; the exorcists' attempt vain, to be exposed (v.15-16). The witnesses observe Matthew Henry's note on the conquest of Satan: the gospel wars against Satan, and here is the conquest of Satan even in those voluntarily devoted to him (the exorcists, the magic-users); the exposure of the false exorcists a triumph of the gospel over the magic of Ephesus; Christ conquering even in the realm of the magicians. The expositors note the contrast with Paul: Paul cast out spirits by the power of God, in true faith; the exorcists tried to borrow the name without faith; the difference between the true servant and the presumptuous impostor; the name effectual to faith, vain to presumption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the exorcists' attempt, the misuse of the name as a charm, and the presumption of using it without faith. The verse thus records the exorcists' attempt to use the name of Jesus — adjuring by Jesus whom Paul preacheth — teaching the presumption of using the name as a charm, without faith, soon to be exposed.
14:
"And there were seven sons of one Sceva, a Jew, and chief of the priests, which did so." The witnesses note the seven sons of Sceva. Barnes notes Sceva is a Greek name; "chief of the priests" cannot mean he was high priest (improbable that his sons would be wandering exorcists), but that he was of the sacerdotal order, a Jewish chief priest, of distinction. Gill notes they strolled about and used exorcisms. The witnesses note the seven sons of Sceva, a Jewish priest, among the wandering exorcists who attempted to use the name of Jesus — men of priestly family, yet dealing in magic and presumption. The witnesses note the sons of Sceva. The witnesses note the priestly family in magic.

The witnesses note the seven sons of Sceva, of a priestly family — yet wandering exorcists dealing in magic.

So the witnesses make the verse the seven sons of Sceva. The lesson the expositors draw is the degradation of a priestly family into magic and the presumption of the exorcists. The witnesses note the men: "seven sons of one Sceva, a Jew, and chief of the priests, which did so" — seven sons of Sceva, a Jewish priest, were among the exorcists who attempted to use the name of Jesus; Barnes notes Sceva is a Greek name, and "chief of the priests" means not the high priest (improbable that his sons would be wandering exorcists), but one of the sacerdotal order, a chief priest of distinction. The expositors observe the degradation: these were sons of a priest, of a priestly family — men who should have known and served the true God; yet they were wandering exorcists, dealing in magic and conjuration; the degradation of a priestly family into superstition and imposture; the abuse of their heritage. The witnesses press the presumption: these men, of priestly descent, attempted to use the name of Jesus as a charm (v.13); the presumption of using the name without faith; the priestly family, ignorant of true faith, trying to borrow the power of the name; the misuse of their position and of the name. The expositors draw the lesson on the abuse of religious heritage: the sons of Sceva, of priestly family, were yet far from God, dealing in magic; the heritage no proof of grace; the priestly descent abused; men may have the highest religious connections and yet be strangers to true faith. The witnesses observe the number seven: seven sons, all engaged in the imposture; the whole family in the magic arts; the priestly house given to exorcism; the degradation complete. The expositors note the contrast with the true priesthood: these sons of a priest dealt in magic, while the gospel offered the true power of God; the false priesthood and the true power; the contrast of the magic-using priests with the apostle of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sons of Sceva, the degradation of the priestly family, and the presumption of the exorcists. The verse thus records the seven sons of Sceva — of a priestly family, yet wandering exorcists — teaching the degradation of a priestly family into magic and the presumption of using the name without faith.
15:
"And the evil spirit answered and said, Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?" The witnesses note the evil spirit's answer. Barnes paraphrases: "Jesus I know — his power to cast out devils I know; Paul I know — Paul's power to cast out devils; but who are ye? — what power have you over evil spirits? you belong neither to Jesus nor Paul; you are not of their party, and have no right to attempt to expel them in their name." The witnesses note the demon acknowledging Jesus and Paul, but defying the exorcists — exposing their presumption; the spirit knew the true power of Jesus and his servant, but the impostors had no authority. The witnesses note the spirit's answer. The witnesses note the exorcists exposed.

The witnesses note the demon acknowledging Jesus and Paul but defying the exorcists — exposing their presumption.

So the witnesses make the verse the evil spirit's answer. The lesson the expositors draw is the exposure of the exorcists' presumption, the demon acknowledging the true power of Jesus and Paul but defying the impostors. The witnesses note the answer: "Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?" — the evil spirit, through the possessed man, answered the exorcists; Barnes paraphrases: Jesus I know — his power to cast out devils; Paul I know — Paul's power; but who are ye? — what power have you? you belong neither to Jesus nor Paul, and have no right to use their name. The expositors observe the demon's acknowledgment: the evil spirit knew Jesus and Paul — the power of Jesus and the authority of his servant Paul; the demon recognizing the true power of the Lord and the genuine apostle; even the powers of darkness knowing the true authority. The witnesses press the defiance of the exorcists: "but who are ye?" — the spirit defied the impostors, who had no authority over it; they were not of Jesus' party, not his servants, but presumptuous borrowers of his name; the demon exposing their lack of authority; the impostors unmasked. The expositors draw the lesson on the exposure of presumption: the exorcists, using the name without faith or authority, were exposed by the very demon they sought to expel; the presumption unmasked; the name effectual only to the true servant, vain to the impostor; the false exorcists shown to have no power. The witnesses observe the testimony to the true power: the demon's words testify to the real power of Jesus and the genuine authority of Paul — over against the impostors; the powers of darkness acknowledging the true Lord and his servant, while defying the false; the contrast of the true and the false. The expositors note the irony: the demon, an enemy, bore witness to Jesus and Paul, and exposed the impostors; the truth borne even by the evil spirit; the false exorcists condemned by the demon's own testimony; the irony of the spirit's witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the spirit's answer, the demon acknowledging Jesus and Paul, and the exposure of the exorcists' presumption. The verse thus records the evil spirit's answer — Jesus I know, Paul I know, but who are ye? — teaching the exposure of the exorcists' presumption and the demon's acknowledgment of the true power of Jesus and his servant.
16:
"And the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them, and overcame them, and prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded." The witnesses note the violent overpowering of the exorcists. Clarke marks the wonder: one man, possessed, was more powerful than the seven brothers, stripping them of their garments and wounding them all — "was not this a proof that he derived his strength from the evil spirit?" Barnes notes the extraordinary rage and power of the possessed (as Mark 5:3). The witnesses note the demoniac leaping on the exorcists, overcoming and wounding them, so that they fled naked and wounded — the presumptuous impostors put to shame, the power of the demon over those who misused the name. The witnesses note the overpowering. The witnesses note the exorcists fleeing.

The witnesses note the demoniac overpowering the exorcists — the presumptuous impostors fleeing naked and wounded.

So the witnesses make the verse the violent overpowering of the exorcists. The lesson the expositors draw is the shameful defeat of the presumptuous impostors, overpowered by the demon they sought to expel. The witnesses note the overpowering: "the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them, and overcame them, and prevailed against them" — the possessed man, energized by the demon, leaped on the exorcists, overcame them, and prevailed; Barnes notes the extraordinary rage and power of the possessed (as Mark 5:3); the demoniac's supernatural strength overpowering the seven. The expositors observe Clarke's marking of the wonder: one man, possessed, was more powerful than the seven brothers — stripping them of their garments and wounding them all; was not this a proof that he derived his strength from the evil spirit? the supernatural power of the demon over the impostors. The witnesses press the shameful flight: "they fled out of that house naked and wounded" — the exorcists fled, stripped of their clothes and wounded; the presumptuous impostors put to shame, beaten and exposed; the public, humiliating defeat; the false exorcists disgraced. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of misusing the name: the exorcists, using the name of Jesus without faith or authority, were not protected but overpowered; the name no charm for the unbeliever; the presumption met with shameful defeat; the danger of trifling with holy things; the impostors punished. The witnesses observe the contrast with Paul: Paul cast out the spirit (Acts 16:18) by the true power of God; the exorcists were overpowered by the demon; the difference between the true servant, whom the demon obeyed, and the impostors, whom it overpowered; the name effectual to faith, dangerous to presumption. The expositors note the public exposure: the exorcists fled naked and wounded, the event known to all (v.17); the public disgrace of the impostors a witness to the true power of Jesus and the danger of misusing it; the defeat manifest, the lesson plain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the overpowering of the exorcists, the shameful flight, and the danger of misusing the name without faith. The verse thus records the violent overpowering of the exorcists — fleeing naked and wounded — teaching the shameful defeat of the presumptuous impostors and the danger of misusing the name without faith.
17:
"And this was known to all the Jews and Greeks also dwelling at Ephesus; and fear fell on them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified." The witnesses note the fear and the magnifying of the name. Barnes notes the transaction showed that the miracles wrought by Paul in the name of Jesus were real, wrought in attestation of the truth; impostors could not work such miracles, and they who pretended to exposed themselves; "the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified" — acquired increasing honour. The witnesses note the defeat of the exorcists becoming known to all, Jews and Greeks, so that fear fell on all and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified — the exposure of the false honouring the true; the gospel advanced by the very event. The witnesses note the fear. The witnesses note the name magnified.

The witnesses note fear falling on all and the name of Jesus magnified — the exposure of the false honouring the true.

So the witnesses make the verse the fear and the magnifying of the name. The lesson the expositors draw is that the defeat of the false exorcists honoured the true name of Jesus and advanced the gospel. The witnesses note the spread of the event: "this was known to all the Jews and Greeks also dwelling at Ephesus" — the defeat of the exorcists became known to all, Jews and Gentiles; the event publicly known, spread through the city. The expositors observe the fear: "fear fell on them all" — a holy awe and dread fell on all; the sense of the power of God and the danger of trifling with holy things; the fear of God produced by the event; the awe that follows the manifestation of divine power. The witnesses press the magnifying of the name: "the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified" — the name of Jesus acquired increasing honour; Barnes notes the transaction showed that Paul's miracles in the name of Jesus were real, wrought in attestation of the truth, while the impostors who pretended to the power exposed themselves; the contrast honouring the true name; the name magnified by the exposure of the false. The expositors draw the lesson on the honouring of the true by the exposure of the false: the defeat of the false exorcists, who misused the name, magnified the true name of Jesus; the contrast between the impostors' shameful defeat and the real power in Paul's hands honouring the Lord; the false exposed, the true exalted; the name magnified. The witnesses observe the advance of the gospel by the event: the fear and the magnifying of the name advanced the gospel — leading to the confession and the burning of the books (v.18-19); the very event the enemies might have hoped would discredit the gospel turned to its advance; the providence overruling for the gospel's good. The expositors note the fitness of the fear: the fear was wholesome — a holy awe of the power of God, a dread of trifling with the name; the fear that prepares for repentance; the awe leading to the confession and forsaking of sin (v.18-19); the salutary fear. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fear falling on all, the name of Jesus magnified, and the gospel advanced by the exposure of the false. The verse thus records the fear and the magnifying of the name — the defeat of the false honouring the true — teaching that the exposure of the false exorcists honoured the name of Jesus and advanced the gospel.
18:
"And many that believed came, and confessed, and shewed their deeds." The witnesses note the confession of the new believers. Barnes notes "their deeds" — their evil course of life, the magical arts they had used, and iniquity in general; and draws the lesson: "they who make a profession of religion will be willing to confess their transgressions; and no man can have evidence that he is truly renewed who is not willing to confess as well as to forsake his sins" (Romans 10:10; Proverbs 28:13). The witnesses note many believers coming, confessing, and showing their deeds — the fruit of true conversion in the open confession and forsaking of sin; the magic-users renouncing their arts. The witnesses note the confession. The witnesses note the fruit of conversion.

The witnesses note the believers confessing and showing their deeds — the fruit of true conversion in confessing and forsaking sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the confession of the new believers. The lesson the expositors draw is the fruit of true conversion in the open confession and forsaking of sin. The witnesses note the confession: "many that believed came, and confessed, and shewed their deeds" — many who believed came and confessed, openly showing their deeds; Barnes notes "their deeds" were their evil course of life, the magical arts they had used, and iniquity in general; the believers confessing their former sins. The expositors observe Barnes's lesson on confession as the mark of true conversion: they who make a true profession of religion will be willing to confess their transgressions; and no man can have evidence that he is truly renewed who is not willing to confess as well as to forsake his sins; "with the heart man believeth... and with the mouth confession is made" (Romans 10:10); "he that covereth his sins shall not prosper; but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy" (Proverbs 28:13); confession the mark of the penitent. The witnesses press the openness of the confession: "confessed, and shewed their deeds" — they not only confessed in general, but openly showed their deeds, their former practices; the full, open confession; the magic-users renouncing their arts publicly; the genuine, thorough confession. The expositors draw the lesson on the fruit of conversion: the new believers, convicted, confessed and forsook their sins; the fruit of true conversion in the open confession and renunciation of sin; the changed life following the changed heart; the magic-users turning from their arts; faith working repentance. The witnesses observe the contrast with the false exorcists: the impostors used magic for gain and were exposed; the true converts confessed and forsook their magic; the difference between the false and the true; the genuine conversion shown in the renunciation of sin. The expositors note the necessity of forsaking sin: confession without forsaking is empty; the believers confessed and forsook (the burning of the books, v.19); the true repentance both confesses and forsakes; the fruit of conversion the abandonment of sin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the confession of the new believers, the fruit of true conversion, and the open confessing and forsaking of sin. The verse thus records the confession of the new believers — confessing and showing their deeds — teaching the fruit of true conversion in the open confession and forsaking of sin.
19:
"Many of them also which used curious arts brought their books together, and burned them before all men: and they counted the price of them, and found it fifty thousand pieces of silver." The witnesses note the burning of the magic books. Barnes notes "curious arts" (perierga) denotes the arts of magic, jugglery, and incantation, much practised at Ephesus (the famed "Ephesian letters"); the believers brought their books and burned them, counting the price at fifty thousand pieces of silver. The witnesses note the converted magic-users burning their valuable books of magic — the costly, decisive forsaking of sin; the fruit of repentance in the destruction of the instruments of their former iniquity, at great loss. The witnesses note the burning of the books. The witnesses note the costly forsaking of sin.

The witnesses note the magic books burned at great cost — the costly, decisive forsaking of sin, the fruit of repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse the burning of the magic books. The lesson the expositors draw is the costly, decisive forsaking of sin, the fruit of true repentance. The witnesses note the act: "many of them also which used curious arts brought their books together, and burned them before all men" — the converted magic-users brought their books of magic and burned them publicly; Barnes notes "curious arts" denotes magic, jugglery, and incantation, much practised at Ephesus (the famed "Ephesian letters," charms and spells); the believers destroying the instruments of their former sin. The expositors observe the cost: "they counted the price of them, and found it fifty thousand pieces of silver" — the books were valuable, worth fifty thousand pieces of silver (a great sum); the believers burned them at great loss; the costly forsaking of sin; the renunciation that counted no cost too great. The witnesses press the decisiveness of the act: they "burned them before all men" — publicly, openly, decisively; not selling the books (which would spread the evil), but destroying them; the thorough, final forsaking of sin; the decisive break with the past. The expositors draw the lesson on the costly forsaking of sin: the believers forsook their sin at great cost — burning valuable books; true repentance counts no cost too great to be rid of sin; the willingness to suffer loss rather than keep the instruments of iniquity; "if thy right hand offend thee, cut it off" (Matthew 5:30); the costly, decisive renunciation. The witnesses observe the wisdom of burning rather than selling: they burned the books, not sold them; for to sell would be to spread the evil to others; the conscience that will not profit by sin nor pass it on; the thorough destruction of the evil; the decisive break. The expositors note the public testimony: the books burned before all men — a public testimony to the renunciation of sin and the power of the gospel; the open forsaking a witness to the city; the gospel's triumph over the magic of Ephesus visibly displayed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the burning of the magic books, the costly forsaking of sin, and the decisive, thorough renunciation. The verse thus records the burning of the magic books — at great cost, before all men — teaching the costly, decisive forsaking of sin, the fruit of true repentance.
20:
"So mightily grew the word of God and prevailed." The witnesses note the mighty growth of the word. Gill notes the word grew and prevailed "over conjuring books, conjurers themselves, yea, even the devils; the power of God going along with it, many were converted... and were delivered from the power of darkness... and translated into the kingdom of Christ." Barnes notes "so mightily" — so powerfully; the power that made men willing to give up their gains and renounce imposture. The witnesses note the word of God growing mightily and prevailing — over magic, magicians, and demons; the triumph of the gospel in Ephesus; the power of God prevailing. The witnesses note the mighty growth. The witnesses note the word prevailing.

The witnesses note the word of God growing mightily and prevailing — over magic, magicians, and demons, the gospel's triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse the mighty growth of the word. The lesson the expositors draw is the triumph of the gospel in Ephesus, the word of God prevailing over all opposition. The witnesses note the growth: "so mightily grew the word of God and prevailed" — the word of God grew mightily and prevailed; the gospel triumphing, spreading, conquering; the powerful advance of the word. The expositors observe Gill's note on what it prevailed over: the word prevailed over conjuring books (the burned magic books), conjurers themselves (the converted magic-users), and even the devils (the spirits cast out, the exorcists exposed); the power of God going along with it, many converted, delivered from the power of darkness, translated into the kingdom of Christ; the triumph over all the powers of darkness and deception. The witnesses press Barnes's note on "so mightily": so powerfully; the power that made men willing to give up their gains, renounce imposture, and burn their valuable books; the mighty power of the word, overcoming the love of money, the bondage of magic, the power of Satan; the word prevailing against all. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the word: the word of God is mighty, prevailing over all opposition — magic, greed, demons, deception; "the weapons of our warfare are mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds" (2 Corinthians 10:4); the gospel triumphant; the word conquering. The witnesses observe the triumph in Ephesus: the gospel, in a city famed for magic and the worship of Diana, prevailed — over the magic, the magicians, the demons; the word triumphing in the stronghold of darkness; the gospel's victory in Ephesus. The expositors note the means and the power: the word, attended by the power of God, prevailed; not the eloquence of the preacher, but the power of God in the word; the Spirit's power making the word effectual; the word mighty through God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the mighty growth of the word, the triumph of the gospel over all opposition, and the power of God prevailing. The verse thus records the mighty growth of the word — prevailing over magic, magicians, and demons — teaching the triumph of the gospel in Ephesus and the power of the word of God prevailing over all opposition.
21:
"After these things were ended, Paul purposed in the spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem, saying, After I have been there, I must also see Rome." The witnesses note Paul's purpose to go to Jerusalem and Rome. Chrysostom notes Paul "purposed in the Spirit," showing that all he did was done not of his own power; and prophesies, "I must also see Rome" — to comfort and arouse the disciples; at this point he wrote his Epistle to the Corinthians. The witnesses note Paul, after the Ephesian ministry, purposing in the Spirit to pass through Macedonia and Achaia to Jerusalem, and then to Rome — the divine direction of his plans, the prophetic purpose to reach the imperial city. The witnesses note Paul's purpose. The witnesses note the purpose to see Rome.

The witnesses note Paul purposing in the Spirit to go to Jerusalem and Rome — the divine direction of his plans.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's purpose to go to Jerusalem and Rome. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine direction of Paul's plans and his prophetic purpose to reach Rome. The witnesses note the purpose: "Paul purposed in the spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem" — Paul formed the purpose to revisit the churches of Macedonia and Achaia, then to go to Jerusalem (with the collection for the poor); the apostle's plan for his journey. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on "in the spirit": Paul purposed "in the Spirit," showing that all he did was done not of his own power, but by the direction of the Spirit; whether "in his own spirit/mind" (resolving) or "by the Holy Spirit" (directed), the purpose was under the Spirit's guidance; the divine direction of his plans. The witnesses press the purpose to see Rome: "After I have been there, I must also see Rome" — Paul declares his purpose, even his necessity ("I must"), to see Rome; Chrysostom notes this was a kind of prophecy, to comfort and arouse the disciples; the apostle's longstanding desire (Romans 1:11-15) to reach the imperial city; the prophetic purpose. The expositors draw the lesson on the "I must": Paul felt a divine necessity to see Rome — not mere desire, but a constraint; the providence drawing him to the capital of the empire; the gospel to reach Rome, the center of the world; the divine purpose in his journey (fulfilled, though by a way he did not expect — as a prisoner, Acts 28). The witnesses observe the providence in Paul's plans: his purpose was under the Spirit's direction; the apostle planning, yet led by God; the journey to Jerusalem and Rome ordered by providence; the man proposing, the Spirit directing (as the restraints of Acts 16:6-7). The expositors note Chrysostom's observation that Paul wrote to the Corinthians at this point: the historical setting of the epistle; the apostle's letters arising from his journeys; the providence ordering the writing as the travels. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's purpose, the divine direction of his plans, and the prophetic purpose to see Rome. The verse thus records Paul's purpose to go to Jerusalem and Rome — purposing in the Spirit — teaching the divine direction of his plans and the prophetic purpose to reach the imperial city.
22:
"So he sent into Macedonia two of them that ministered unto him, Timotheus and Erastus; but he himself stayed in Asia for a season." The witnesses note the sending of Timothy and Erastus. Barnes notes Timothy was a proper person to visit the churches, having been there before (Acts 16-17); and Erastus was the treasurer of Corinth (Romans 16:23), "a very proper person to be sent... for the purpose of making the collection for the poor at Jerusalem"; Paul had wisdom to employ a man accustomed to money matters. The witnesses note Paul sending Timothy and Erastus ahead into Macedonia, while he himself remained in Asia — the wise ordering of the mission, the fit men sent for the work. The witnesses note the sending of helpers. The witnesses note the fit men for the work.

The witnesses note Paul sending Timothy and Erastus ahead — the wise ordering of the mission, the fit men for the work.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of Timothy and Erastus. The lesson the expositors draw is the wise ordering of the mission, the fit men chosen for the work. The witnesses note the sending: "he sent into Macedonia two of them that ministered unto him, Timotheus and Erastus" — Paul sent two of his helpers ahead into Macedonia, to prepare for his coming and to advance the work (and the collection); the apostle ordering his helpers, sending forerunners. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the fitness of the men: Timothy was a proper person to visit the churches, having been there before (Acts 16-17), known and trusted; and Erastus was the treasurer of Corinth (Romans 16:23), a very proper person for making the collection for the poor at Jerusalem; Paul had the wisdom to employ a man accustomed to money matters for the financial work; the fit men for the tasks. The witnesses press the wisdom of the choice: Paul chose men suited to the work — Timothy for the visiting and establishing of the churches, Erastus (a treasurer) for the collection; the wise ordering that matches the man to the task; the prudence of the apostle. The expositors draw the lesson on the wise ordering of the mission: Paul did not do all himself, but sent fit helpers; the delegation of the work to suitable men; the wisdom of employing each according to his gift and experience; the mission ordered with prudence. The witnesses observe Paul's remaining in Asia: "he himself stayed in Asia for a season" — Paul remained at Ephesus a while longer (his work there not yet done, and the riot soon to come); the apostle continuing his labour while his helpers went ahead; the ordering of the work, some sent, some staying. The expositors note the cooperation of the labourers: Paul, Timothy, Erastus, and others, working together; the mission a cooperative work; the apostle and his helpers labouring in concert; the body working together. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sending of Timothy and Erastus, the fit men for the work, and the wise ordering of the mission. The verse thus records the sending of Timothy and Erastus — the fit men sent ahead — teaching the wise ordering of the mission and the choosing of suitable men for the work.
23:
"And the same time there arose no small stir about that way." The witnesses note the rise of the great Ephesian tumult. Gill notes "that way" means the Christian religion, the doctrines and ordinances of the gospel, which the saints were directed to walk in; and that the stir was about how great the gospel grew and how many embraced it. Barnes notes "no small stir" — no little excitement, disturbance, or tumult. The witnesses note that at this time a great tumult arose concerning "that way" (the gospel) — the riot of Demetrius and the silversmiths, provoked by the gospel's success against the worship of Diana; the opposition the success of the gospel stirred. The witnesses note the tumult. The witnesses note the stir about the gospel.

The witnesses note a great tumult arising about "that way" — the riot provoked by the gospel's success against idolatry.

So the witnesses make the verse the rise of the great Ephesian tumult. The lesson the expositors draw is the opposition the gospel's success stirred, the tumult provoked by its advance against idolatry. The witnesses note the tumult: "there arose no small stir about that way" — a great commotion, disturbance, tumult arose concerning "that way"; Barnes notes "no small stir" means no little excitement or tumult; Gill notes "that way" is the Christian religion, the gospel; the great Ephesian riot beginning. The expositors observe the cause of the stir: the gospel had grown mightily and prevailed (v.20), drawing many from idolatry; the success of the gospel against the worship of Diana provoked the opposition; the tumult arising from the gospel's advance; the riot a reaction to the gospel's success. The witnesses press the connection of success and opposition: the gospel's very success (the burning of the books, the turning from idolatry) provoked the tumult; the more the gospel prevailed, the more its enemies were stirred; the opposition a measure of the success; the riot the reaction to the advance. The expositors draw the lesson on the opposition the gospel meets: the advance of the gospel provokes the enemies, especially where it touches their interests (here, the trade in idols); the gospel's success stirring the opposition; "a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries" (1 Corinthians 16:9); the open door and the adversaries together. The witnesses observe the significance of "that way": the gospel called "the way" (as Acts 9:2; 24:14) — the way of life, of salvation, of walking with God; the gospel a way, a manner of life; the tumult against "the way" the opposition to the gospel. The expositors note the providence in the tumult: the riot, though a great opposition, would be overruled — the town clerk quieting it (v.35-41), the gospel not stopped; the providence preserving the apostle and the church; the opposition not prevailing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the great tumult, the opposition the gospel's success stirred, and the riot against "the way." The verse thus records the rise of the great Ephesian tumult — about "that way" — teaching the opposition the gospel's success stirred, the riot provoked by its advance against idolatry.
24:
"For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, which made silver shrines for Diana, brought no small gain unto the craftsmen;" The witnesses note Demetrius and the trade in silver shrines. Barnes notes Demetrius was a silversmith who made "silver shrines" (small models or temples of Diana, sold to worshippers and pilgrims), bringing much gain to the craftsmen; his complaint (v.26-27) showing the trade depended on the worship of Diana. Gill notes he made silver vessels and images. The witnesses note Demetrius, a silversmith whose trade in silver shrines of Diana brought much gain to the craftsmen — the love of money, threatened by the gospel, the root of the coming riot. The witnesses note Demetrius. The witnesses note the trade in shrines.

The witnesses note Demetrius and the lucrative trade in silver shrines of Diana — the love of money, the root of the coming riot.

So the witnesses make the verse Demetrius and the trade in silver shrines. The lesson the expositors draw is the love of money, threatened by the gospel, as the root of the coming riot. The witnesses note the man and his trade: "a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, which made silver shrines for Diana" — Demetrius, a silversmith, made silver shrines for Diana; Barnes notes these were small models or temples of Diana, sold to worshippers and pilgrims; Gill notes silver vessels and images; the trade in idolatrous objects. The expositors observe the gain: "brought no small gain unto the craftsmen" — the trade brought much gain to Demetrius and his fellow-craftsmen; the lucrative business in the shrines of Diana; the profit from idolatry; the love of money at stake. The witnesses press the threat the gospel posed: the gospel, turning men from idolatry (v.26), threatened the trade; if men ceased to worship Diana, they would cease to buy the shrines; the craftsmen's gain threatened by the gospel; the love of money roused. The expositors draw the lesson on the love of money as the root of the riot: the coming tumult sprang not from zeal for Diana (though that was the pretext), but from the love of money — the threatened gain of the craftsmen; "the love of money is the root of all evil" (1 Timothy 6:10); the riot rooted in avarice; the gain at stake the true motive. The witnesses observe the conflict of the gospel and the love of money: the gospel, by turning men from idolatry, struck at the lucrative trade; the love of money opposing the gospel; the conflict of the kingdom of God and avarice; the gospel threatening the gain of the idol-makers. The expositors note the pretext and the motive: the pretext would be zeal for Diana and the city's honour (v.27-28), but the motive was the love of money; the avarice cloaked under religious zeal; the true motive the gain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Demetrius and his trade, the love of money threatened by the gospel, and the root of the coming riot. The verse thus records Demetrius and the trade in silver shrines — the lucrative business in Diana's shrines — teaching the love of money, threatened by the gospel, as the root of the coming riot.
25:
"Whom he called together with the workmen of like occupation, and said, Sirs, ye know that by this craft we have our wealth." The witnesses note Demetrius gathering the craftsmen and appealing to their gain. Barnes notes he called together those in his employ and all engaged in the same business, who would be affected the same way; "by this craft we have our wealth" — our acquisition, our property; they were dependent on it for a living. Gill notes he called the chief of the trade, the masters. The witnesses note Demetrius assembling the craftsmen and openly appealing to their common interest — their livelihood threatened by the gospel; the love of money the first and frankest argument. The witnesses note the gathering of the craftsmen. The witnesses note the appeal to gain.

The witnesses note Demetrius gathering the craftsmen and appealing to their threatened livelihood — the love of money the first argument.
