So the witnesses make the verse Demetrius's gathering of the craftsmen. The lesson the expositors draw is the frank appeal to self-interest, the love of money the first argument against the gospel. The witnesses note the gathering: "he called together with the workmen of like occupation" — Demetrius assembled the craftsmen, those in his employ and all engaged in the same business; Barnes notes they would be affected the same way, so easy to excite; Gill notes he called the chief of the trade; the rallying of the interested party. The expositors observe the frank appeal to gain: "Sirs, ye know that by this craft we have our wealth" — Demetrius openly appeals to their common interest, their livelihood; Barnes notes "our wealth" means their acquisition, their property, on which they depended for a living; the frank appeal to self-interest; the love of money the first argument. The witnesses press the openness of the motive at the start: Demetrius begins with the gain — "by this craft we have our wealth"; the true motive (the threatened livelihood) stated plainly to the craftsmen, before the religious pretext (v.27); the love of money the real and frankest argument; the avarice owned among themselves. The expositors draw the lesson on the love of money opposing the gospel: the craftsmen's opposition sprang from their threatened gain; the love of money the root; the frank appeal to self-interest the first argument; "they all seek their own" (Philippians 2:21); the avarice rousing the opposition. The witnesses observe the craft of Demetrius's appeal: he begins with the gain (frank among the craftsmen), then cloaks it under zeal for Diana and the city (v.27); the true motive first, the religious pretext after; the demagogue's craft, stirring the interest then the passion. The expositors note the contrast with the gospel: the gospel turned men from idolatry to God; the craftsmen turned men against the gospel for gain; the love of God and the love of money in conflict; the gospel and the avarice opposed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Demetrius gathering the craftsmen, the frank appeal to gain, and the love of money opposing the gospel. The verse thus records Demetrius gathering the craftsmen — appealing to their threatened wealth — teaching the frank appeal to self-interest, the love of money the first argument against the gospel.
26:
"Moreover ye see and hear, that not alone at Ephesus, but almost throughout all Asia, this Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people, saying that they be no gods, which are made with hands:" The witnesses note Demetrius's testimony to the gospel's success. Barnes marks the irony: "we have here the noble testimony of a heathen to the zeal and success of the ministry of Paul" — an acknowledgment that his labours had turned away much people, not only at Ephesus but throughout Asia, from idolatry. The witnesses note Demetrius, complaining, bearing unwitting testimony to the gospel's wide success — Paul persuading and turning away much people from the worship of idols made with hands; the enemy confessing the gospel's triumph. The witnesses note the testimony to success. The witnesses note no gods made with hands.

The witnesses note Demetrius unwittingly testifying to the gospel's wide success — Paul turning many from idols made with hands.

So the witnesses make the verse Demetrius's testimony to the gospel's success. The lesson the expositors draw is the unwitting testimony of the enemy to the gospel's triumph and the truth that idols are no gods. The witnesses note the complaint: "this Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people, saying that they be no gods, which are made with hands" — Demetrius complains that Paul had persuaded and turned away much people from idolatry, teaching that idols made with hands are no gods. The expositors observe Barnes's marking of the irony: here is the noble testimony of a heathen to the zeal and success of Paul's ministry — an acknowledgment, from an enemy, that his labours had turned away much people, not only at Ephesus but throughout all Asia, from the worship of idols; the enemy confessing the gospel's wide triumph. The witnesses press the breadth of the success testified: "not alone at Ephesus, but almost throughout all Asia" — the gospel had turned many from idolatry, not in Ephesus only, but throughout the province; the wide success, confessed by the enemy; the gospel's triumph attested by its opponent. The expositors draw the lesson on the enemy's unwitting testimony: Demetrius, complaining, bore witness to the gospel's success; the enemy's accusation a confession of the truth; the opposition itself a testimony to the gospel's power; the foe confessing the triumph. The witnesses observe the truth Paul taught: "they be no gods, which are made with hands" — Paul taught the great truth that idols made with hands are no gods; the truth against idolatry; "an idol is nothing in the world" (1 Corinthians 8:4); the gospel exposing the folly of idols; the truth that gods made by men are no gods. The expositors note the success of this teaching: the truth that idols are no gods turned many from idolatry; the gospel's power to deliver men from the worship of idols; the truth prevailing against the deception; the people turned to the true God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Demetrius's testimony to the gospel's success, the truth that idols are no gods, and the enemy's unwitting confession of the triumph. The verse thus records Demetrius's testimony to the gospel's success — Paul turning many from idols made with hands — teaching the enemy's unwitting testimony to the gospel's triumph and the truth that idols are no gods.
27:
"So that not only this our craft is in danger to be set at nought; but also that the temple of the great goddess Diana should be despised, and her magnificence should be destroyed, whom all Asia and the world worshippeth." The witnesses note Demetrius cloaking his gain under zeal for Diana. Gill notes the craft would be brought into contempt if men believed the images had no divinity; and the temple and worship of Diana despised. The witnesses note Demetrius, having appealed to gain (v.25-26), now cloaking the threat under concern for the temple and honour of Diana — the religious pretext masking the love of money; the appeal to civic and religious pride. The witnesses note the religious pretext. The witnesses note the appeal to Diana's honour.

The witnesses note Demetrius cloaking his threatened gain under zeal for Diana — the religious pretext masking the love of money.

So the witnesses make the verse Demetrius cloaking his gain under zeal for Diana. The lesson the expositors draw is the religious pretext masking the love of money, the appeal to civic and religious pride. The witnesses note the double threat Demetrius names: "not only this our craft is in danger to be set at nought; but also that the temple of the great goddess Diana should be despised" — first the craft (the gain), then the temple and honour of Diana; the threat to both the trade and the worship. The expositors observe the cloaking of the motive: having frankly appealed to gain among the craftsmen (v.25-26), Demetrius now cloaks the threat under concern for the temple and honour of Diana; the religious pretext added to the financial motive; the love of money masked under zeal for the goddess; the demagogue's craft. The witnesses press the appeal to pride: "her magnificence should be destroyed, whom all Asia and the world worshippeth" — Demetrius appeals to civic and religious pride — the great goddess Diana, worshipped by all Asia and the world, her temple (one of the wonders of the world) and her magnificence threatened; the appeal to the people's pride in their goddess and her temple; the rallying of religious zeal and civic honour. The expositors draw the lesson on the religious pretext: the love of money, the real motive (v.25), is cloaked under zeal for religion; the avarice masked under piety; the demagogue stirring religious and civic pride to serve his gain; the pretext of religion covering the love of money; the false zeal. The witnesses observe the craft of the appeal: Demetrius moves from the gain (frank among the craftsmen) to the temple and the goddess (the public pretext); the private motive and the public cause; the avarice clothed in zeal; the skillful rousing of the mob's passion under a religious banner. The expositors note the irony of the threat: Demetrius fears Diana's magnificence "should be destroyed" — yet a god that can be destroyed by men turning away is no god; the very fear exposing the vanity of the idol; the idol that needs defending no true god. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Demetrius cloaking his gain under zeal for Diana, the religious pretext masking the love of money, and the appeal to pride. The verse thus records Demetrius cloaking his gain under zeal for Diana — the temple and honour threatened — teaching the religious pretext masking the love of money and the appeal to civic and religious pride.
28:
"And when they heard these sayings, they were full of wrath, and cried out, saying, Great is Diana of the Ephesians." The witnesses note the craftsmen's rage and the cry for Diana. Barnes notes they were full of wrath, "probably at the prospect of losing their gains"; the term "great" was often applied to Diana by the Greeks; the design of the clamour was to raise a persecution against Paul and secure their employment; "often, when men have no arguments, they raise a clamour." The witnesses note the craftsmen, enraged at the threat to their gain, crying out "Great is Diana of the Ephesians" — the clamour of greed cloaked in religious zeal; the mob roused. The witnesses note the wrath. The witnesses note the cry for Diana.

The witnesses note the craftsmen enraged, crying "Great is Diana" — the clamour of greed cloaked in religious zeal.

So the witnesses make the verse the craftsmen's rage and the cry for Diana. The lesson the expositors draw is the clamour of greed cloaked in religious zeal, the mob roused by passion when arguments fail. The witnesses note the wrath: "when they heard these sayings, they were full of wrath" — the craftsmen, hearing Demetrius's appeal, were filled with wrath; Barnes notes the wrath was probably at the prospect of losing their gains; the rage of threatened greed. The expositors observe the cry: "cried out, saying, Great is Diana of the Ephesians" — the craftsmen raised the cry for Diana; Barnes notes "great" was often applied to Diana by the Greeks; the design of the clamour was to raise a persecution against Paul and secure their employment; the clamour of the threatened. The witnesses press the cloaking of greed in zeal: the cry "Great is Diana" was the religious banner under which the love of money marched; the real motive (the threatened gain) cloaked in the cry for the goddess; the avarice clamouring under a religious pretext; the greed disguised as zeal. The expositors draw the lesson Barnes marks on clamour without argument: often, when men have no arguments, they raise a clamour; when their employments are in danger of being ruined, they are filled with rage; the craftsmen, having no answer to the gospel, raised a clamour; the cry of passion replacing reason; the mob roused by noise, not argument. The witnesses observe the rousing of the mob: the cry "Great is Diana" was calculated to inflame the people, to stir the religious and civic passion, to raise a tumult against Paul; the demagogue's clamour rousing the mob; the passion stirred, the riot kindled. The expositors note the irony of the cry: "Great is Diana" — yet the very greatness of the goddess was in question; the loud cry asserting what could not be proved; the clamour asserting the idol's greatness against the truth that exposed it; the noise covering the want of argument. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the craftsmen's wrath, the cry for Diana, and the clamour of greed cloaked in religious zeal. The verse thus records the craftsmen's rage and the cry for Diana — Great is Diana of the Ephesians — teaching the clamour of greed cloaked in religious zeal, the mob roused by passion when arguments fail.
29:
"And the whole city was filled with confusion: and having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, men of Macedonia, Paul's companions in travel, they rushed with one accord into the theatre." The witnesses note the spread of the tumult and the seizing of Paul's companions. Gill notes the workmen ran through the city crying out, bringing the people out to inquire, the mob gathering and increasing, throwing the city into confusion. Barnes identifies Gaius (who had entertained Paul, 1 Corinthians 1:14) and Aristarchus (who later attended Paul to Rome, Colossians 4:10). The witnesses note the whole city thrown into confusion, the mob seizing Gaius and Aristarchus (Paul's companions) and rushing into the theatre — the tumult spreading, the innocent companions seized. The witnesses note the confusion. The witnesses note the companions seized.

The witnesses note the city in confusion and the mob seizing Paul's companions, rushing into the theatre.

So the witnesses make the verse the spread of the tumult. The lesson the expositors draw is the spread of the confusion and the seizing of the innocent companions. The witnesses note the confusion: "the whole city was filled with confusion" — Gill notes the workmen ran through the city crying out, bringing the people out to inquire, the mob gathering and increasing, throwing the city into confusion; the tumult spreading through the whole city; the uproar general. The expositors observe the spread of the riot: from the craftsmen's meeting, the cry spread through the city, the mob gathering; the confusion engulfing the whole city; the tumult, kindled by Demetrius, spreading to all; the riot general. The witnesses note the seizing of the companions: "having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, men of Macedonia, Paul's companions in travel, they rushed with one accord into the theatre" — the mob seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul's companions, and rushed into the theatre (the great place of public assembly); Barnes identifies Gaius (who had entertained Paul, 1 Corinthians 1:14) and Aristarchus (who later attended Paul to Rome, Colossians 4:10); the innocent companions seized. The expositors draw the lesson on the suffering of the companions: Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul's companions, were seized by the mob; the innocent suffering for their association with Paul; the companions sharing the apostle's danger; the cost of fellowship with the persecuted. The witnesses observe the rush to the theatre: "rushed with one accord into the theatre" — the mob rushed to the theatre, the great public place; the tumult gathering to a head; the disorderly assembly; the mob's violence. The expositors note the danger to the companions: seized by the enraged mob, dragged into the theatre, in peril of their lives; the danger of the situation; the providence that would preserve them (the riot quieted, v.35-41). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the city in confusion, the seizing of Paul's companions, and the spread of the tumult. The verse thus records the spread of the tumult — the city in confusion, the companions seized and dragged to the theatre — teaching the spread of the confusion and the seizing of the innocent companions.
30:
"And when Paul would have entered in unto the people, the disciples suffered him not." The witnesses note Paul's desire to enter and the disciples' restraint. Gill marks Paul's courage: he would have entered the theatre to preach to the people, to remove their prejudices, show the error of their idolatry, and reconcile them — risking his life; "which shows the apostle's greatness of soul, his firmness, constancy, and intrepidity, and his great concern for his companions." Barnes notes he wished to address them and defend his cause. The witnesses note Paul, courageous, wishing to enter the theatre to face the mob, and the disciples preventing him — the apostle's bravery and the disciples' wise care. The witnesses note Paul's desire to enter. The witnesses note the disciples' restraint.

The witnesses note Paul wishing to face the mob and the disciples restraining him — the apostle's courage and the disciples' wise care.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's desire to enter and the disciples' restraint. The lesson the expositors draw is the courage of Paul and the wise care of the disciples. The witnesses note Paul's desire: "when Paul would have entered in unto the people" — Paul wished to enter the theatre, to face the mob, to address them; Gill notes he would have entered to preach to the people, to remove their prejudices, show the error of their idolatry, and reconcile them — and to defend his companions; the apostle's desire to confront the mob. The expositors observe Paul's courage that Gill marks: his wishing to enter shows his greatness of soul, his firmness, constancy, and intrepidity, and his great concern for his companions, risking his life; the bravery of the apostle, ready to face the enraged mob for the gospel and for his friends; the courage that would confront the danger. The witnesses press the restraint of the disciples: "the disciples suffered him not" — the disciples prevented Paul from entering; they would not let him expose himself to the fury of the mob; the wise care that restrained his courage; the disciples' love and prudence. The expositors draw the lesson on the balance of courage and prudence: Paul's courage would have entered; the disciples' prudence restrained him; courage tempered by wisdom; the brave apostle held back by wise friends, not from cowardice but from prudence; the balance of zeal and discretion. The witnesses observe the danger Paul would have faced: the enraged mob, in confusion, ready for violence (v.32); to enter would be to risk his life uselessly, the mob not in a state to hear reason; the disciples wisely judging the danger; the prudence that saw the futility and peril. The expositors note the providence in the restraint: the disciples' preventing Paul preserved his life for further service; the providence using the disciples' care to keep the apostle safe; the man preserved by the wisdom of his friends; the restraint a means of preservation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's courage, the disciples' wise restraint, and the balance of zeal and prudence. The verse thus records Paul's desire to enter and the disciples' restraint — they suffered him not — teaching the courage of the apostle and the wise care of the disciples.
31:
"And certain of the chief of Asia, which were his friends, sent unto him, desiring him that he would not adventure himself into the theatre." The witnesses note the Asiarchs' friendly warning. Gill identifies "the chief of Asia" as the Asiarchs — not princes or civil magistrates, but officers who presided over the public games and diversions at the theatre, men of rank and influence; some of whom were Paul's friends. The witnesses note that even certain of the Asiarchs — high officers of the province — being Paul's friends, sent to urge him not to venture into the theatre; the friendship and respect Paul had won even among the great, and their care for his safety. The witnesses note the Asiarchs' friendship. The witnesses note the warning.

The witnesses note the Asiarchs, Paul's friends, warning him not to venture into the theatre — the respect he won even among the great.

So the witnesses make the verse the Asiarchs' friendly warning. The lesson the expositors draw is the friendship and respect Paul won even among the great, and their care for his safety. The witnesses note the men: "certain of the chief of Asia, which were his friends" — Gill identifies these as the Asiarchs, high officers of the province who presided over the public games and diversions at the theatre, men of rank and influence; some of whom were Paul's friends. The expositors observe the significance of their friendship: even some of the Asiarchs — men of high rank, presiding over the very theatre where the mob raged — were Paul's friends; the apostle had won friendship and respect even among the great, the influential, the rulers of the province; the gospel reaching, and the apostle respected by, men of rank. The witnesses press the warning: "sent unto him, desiring him that he would not adventure himself into the theatre" — these friends sent to urge Paul not to venture into the theatre; their care for his safety, their friendly counsel; the influential friends warning the apostle of the danger. The expositors draw the lesson on the friendship Paul won: the apostle, though persecuted by the mob, was befriended by the great; the gospel commending him to men of rank; the respect his character and ministry won even among the rulers; the friends in high places. The witnesses observe the providence in these friendships: God gave Paul friends among the influential, who warned and protected him; the providence raising up friends in high places; the apostle preserved by the care of the great; the friends a means of his safety. The expositors note the wisdom of heeding the warning: the warning of the Asiarchs, joined to the disciples' restraint (v.30), kept Paul from the theatre; the apostle preserved by the counsel of his friends; the wisdom of heeding wise and friendly warning; the man kept safe by the care of others. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Asiarchs' friendship, the respect Paul won among the great, and their care for his safety. The verse thus records the Asiarchs' friendly warning — desiring Paul not to venture into the theatre — teaching the friendship and respect Paul won even among the great and their care for his safety.
32:
"Some therefore cried one thing, and some another: for the assembly was confused; and the more part knew not wherefore they were come together." The witnesses note the confusion of the mob. Barnes marks it "an admirable description of a mob, assembled for what purpose they knew not; but agitated by passions, and strifes, and tumults"; the greater part did not even know why they had come together. The witnesses note the disorderly assembly, some crying one thing and some another, the greater part ignorant of the cause — the picture of a mob, moved by passion, not reason; the confusion of the riot. The witnesses note the confused assembly. The witnesses note the ignorant mob.

The witnesses note the confused mob — crying various things, most ignorant of why they came; the picture of a riot.

So the witnesses make the verse the confusion of the mob. The lesson the expositors draw is the picture of a mob, moved by passion not reason, in confusion and ignorance. The witnesses note the disorder: "some therefore cried one thing, and some another: for the assembly was confused" — the mob in the theatre was in confusion, some crying one thing, some another; the disorderly assembly, no unity, no reason, only noise; the confusion of the riot. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the picture of a mob: an admirable description of a mob, assembled for what purpose they knew not, but agitated by passions, strifes, and tumults; the mob moved not by reason but by passion; the picture of disorderly violence. The witnesses press the ignorance of the crowd: "the more part knew not wherefore they were come together" — the greater part of the mob did not even know why they had come together; drawn by the noise and the crowd, not by any understanding of the cause; the ignorance of the mob; the people swept along without knowing why. The expositors draw the lesson on the nature of a mob: a mob is moved by passion, not reason; the people swept along by the crowd, ignorant of the cause, agitated by noise and strife; the disorder and irrationality of the mob; the danger of the unthinking multitude. The witnesses observe the contrast with the truth: the gospel is reasoned, persuasive (v.8); the mob is confused, ignorant, passionate; the difference between the way of truth and the way of the mob; the reason of the gospel against the passion of the riot. The expositors note the providence in the confusion: the very confusion of the mob, ignorant and disorderly, would help to dissipate it (the town clerk able to quiet them, v.35); the riot, having no real cause or unity, the more easily quieted; the providence in the mob's own confusion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the confused assembly, the ignorant mob, and the picture of passion without reason. The verse thus records the confusion of the mob — crying various things, most ignorant of the cause — teaching the picture of a mob, moved by passion not reason, in confusion and ignorance.
33:
"And they drew Alexander out of the multitude, the Jews putting him forward. And Alexander beckoned with the hand, and would have made his defence unto the people." The witnesses note Alexander put forward by the Jews. Gill notes some think this is Alexander the coppersmith, who later did Paul much evil (2 Timothy 4:14); he was a Jew, put forward by the Jews, perhaps to clear them of the charge (the Jews being also enemies of idolatry, and not wishing to be confounded with the Christians). The witnesses note the Jews thrusting Alexander forward, who beckoned to make a defence — the Jews seeking to distance themselves from Paul and the Christians, lest the mob's rage fall on them too. The witnesses note Alexander put forward. The witnesses note the Jews distancing themselves.

The witnesses note the Jews putting Alexander forward to make a defence — distancing themselves from Paul and the Christians.

So the witnesses make the verse Alexander put forward by the Jews. The lesson the expositors draw is the Jews' attempt to distance themselves from Paul and the Christians, lest the mob's rage fall on them. The witnesses note the act: "they drew Alexander out of the multitude, the Jews putting him forward" — Alexander was drawn out and put forward by the Jews; Gill notes some think this is Alexander the coppersmith, who later did Paul much evil (2 Timothy 4:14); a Jew, thrust forward by the Jews to speak. The expositors observe the motive of the Jews: the Jews, being also enemies of idolatry (and so liable to the mob's rage against those who opposed Diana), wished to distance themselves from Paul and the Christians; they put Alexander forward to make a defence — to clear the Jews of the charge, to show they were not the same as the Christians; the self-protective move; the Jews seeking to avoid the mob's wrath. The witnesses note Alexander's attempt: "Alexander beckoned with the hand, and would have made his defence unto the people" — Alexander beckoned for silence, intending to make a defence; whether for the Jews (to distance them from the Christians) or against Paul; the attempt to address the mob. The expositors draw the lesson on the Jews' self-interest: the Jews, in danger from the mob, sought to protect themselves, distancing themselves from Paul; the self-protective move, not the defence of the truth; the Jews caring for their own safety, not the gospel; the difference between the bold apostle and the self-protective Jews. The witnesses observe the futility of the attempt (the next verse): when the mob perceived Alexander was a Jew, they would not hear him, but cried out the more (v.34); the attempt failing, the mob's rage indiscriminate; the Jews' self-protection vain. The expositors note the providence in the confusion: the Jews' attempt and its failure added to the confusion, helping to dissipate the riot; the providence in the disorder; the riot the more confused, the more easily quieted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Alexander put forward, the Jews distancing themselves, and the self-protective move. The verse thus records Alexander put forward by the Jews — to make a defence — teaching the Jews' attempt to distance themselves from Paul and the Christians, lest the mob's rage fall on them.
34:
"But when they knew that he was a Jew, all with one voice about the space of two hours cried out, Great is Diana of the Ephesians." The witnesses note the two-hour cry of the mob. Barnes notes there was a general prejudice against the Jews, and the mob, perceiving Alexander was a Jew, was unwilling to hear any defence; "with one voice" — unitedly, in one continued shout — "about the space of two hours" they cried out. Gill notes they knew the Jew was equally an enemy to their idolatry. The witnesses note the mob, on learning Alexander was a Jew, refusing to hear him and crying out for two hours "Great is Diana" — the unreasoning frenzy of the mob, the irrational persistence of passion. The witnesses note the two-hour cry. The witnesses note the unreasoning frenzy.

The witnesses note the mob crying "Great is Diana" for two hours — the unreasoning frenzy and irrational persistence of passion.

So the witnesses make the verse the two-hour cry of the mob. The lesson the expositors draw is the unreasoning frenzy of the mob, the irrational persistence of passion. The witnesses note the cause of the refusal: "when they knew that he was a Jew" — Barnes notes there was a general prejudice against the Jews; the mob, perceiving Alexander was a Jew (and the Jews equally enemies of idolatry, Gill notes), was unwilling to hear any defence; the prejudice silencing him. The expositors observe the cry: "all with one voice about the space of two hours cried out, Great is Diana of the Ephesians" — the mob, unitedly, in one continued shout, cried out for about two hours; the persistent, frenzied cry; the mob possessed by passion. The witnesses press the irrationality and persistence: for two hours the mob cried the same thing — "Great is Diana"; no reason, no argument, only the repeated cry of passion; the unreasoning frenzy; the irrational persistence; the mob in the grip of zeal and prejudice. The expositors draw the lesson on the frenzy of the mob: the mob, moved by passion and prejudice, cried for two hours without reason; the irrationality of the inflamed multitude; the persistence of passion when reason is gone; the picture of zeal without knowledge; the frenzy of the deceived. The witnesses observe the contrast with the truth: the gospel reasons and persuades (v.8); the mob shouts and persists in frenzy; the difference between the way of truth and the way of the mob; the reason of the gospel against the unreasoning cry. The expositors note the vanity of the cry: "Great is Diana" — the mob asserting the greatness of an idol that was no god; the loud, persistent cry asserting what was false; the frenzy in defence of a vanity; the zeal for an idol; the noise covering the emptiness of the cause. The witnesses observe the providence in the spent frenzy: the two-hour cry exhausted the mob's passion; the frenzy, spending itself, prepared the way for the town clerk to quiet them (v.35); the providence in the very excess of the riot. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the two-hour cry, the unreasoning frenzy of the mob, and the irrational persistence of passion. The verse thus records the two-hour cry of the mob — Great is Diana — teaching the unreasoning frenzy of the mob and the irrational persistence of passion for a vanity.
35:
"And when the townclerk had appeased the people, he said, Ye men of Ephesus, what man is there that knoweth not how that the city of the Ephesians is a worshipper of the great goddess Diana, and of the image which fell down from Jupiter?" The witnesses note the town clerk's appeasing of the mob. Gill notes this person seems to have been more than a common town clerk — a chief man of the city, a recorder or registrar of high office. The witnesses note the town clerk, a man of authority and prudence, quieting the mob, and beginning his speech by appealing to the well-known devotion of Ephesus to Diana — calming them by acknowledging their zeal, that he might lead them to reason. The witnesses note the town clerk's appeasing. The witnesses note the prudent opening.

The witnesses note the town clerk quieting the mob — calming them by acknowledging their devotion to Diana, to lead them to reason.

So the witnesses make the verse the town clerk's appeasing of the mob. The lesson the expositors draw is the prudent quieting of the mob by a man of authority, beginning by acknowledging their zeal. The witnesses note the man: "the townclerk" — Gill notes he seems to have been more than a common town clerk; a chief man of the city, a recorder or registrar of high office; a man of authority and influence; the official able to quiet the mob. The expositors observe the appeasing: "when the townclerk had appeased the people" — the town clerk, by his authority and prudence, quieted the mob, so that he could be heard; the man of authority calming the frenzy; the prudent official stilling the tumult. The witnesses press the prudent opening of his speech: "Ye men of Ephesus, what man is there that knoweth not how that the city of the Ephesians is a worshipper of the great goddess Diana" — the town clerk begins by acknowledging the well-known devotion of Ephesus to Diana; calming the mob by granting their zeal, assuring them their worship was secure; the wise opening that conciliates before it reasons. The expositors draw the lesson on the prudent handling of the mob: the town clerk, instead of opposing the mob's passion, first acknowledges their devotion (their worship of Diana, the image fallen from Jupiter); calming them by granting what they held dear, that he might then lead them to reason; the wisdom that conciliates before it corrects; the prudent address. The witnesses observe the reference to the image "which fell down from Jupiter": the Ephesians believed their image of Diana had fallen from heaven (from Jupiter); the town clerk grants this belief, to calm them; the appeal to their cherished tradition; the conciliation of their zeal. The expositors note the wisdom of the official's method: he quiets the mob not by argument against their religion, but by assuring them their worship was secure and undisputed; the prudent removal of their fear; the wise leading from passion to reason; the demagogue's frenzy answered by the official's prudence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the town clerk's appeasing, the prudent opening, and the wisdom that calms before it reasons. The verse thus records the town clerk's appeasing of the mob — acknowledging their devotion to Diana — teaching the prudent quieting of the mob by a man of authority, beginning by granting their zeal.
36:
"Seeing then that these things cannot be spoken against, ye ought to be quiet, and to do nothing rashly." The witnesses note the town clerk's call to calm. Gill notes the officer intimates that these things (the city's devotion to Diana) were so certain and well known that nobody would contradict them; therefore they were mistaken in their alarm, and ought to be quiet. Barnes notes the argument — since the zeal of the Ephesians for Diana could not be doubted, there was no danger, and they should do nothing rashly. The witnesses note the town clerk urging the mob to be quiet and act not rashly — since their worship was secure and undisputed, there was no cause for the tumult; the call to calm and deliberation. The witnesses note the call to be quiet. The witnesses note the warning against rashness.

The witnesses note the town clerk urging calm — since the worship was secure, no cause for the tumult, do nothing rashly.

So the witnesses make the verse the town clerk's call to calm. The lesson the expositors draw is the prudent removal of the mob's fear and the warning against rash action. The witnesses note the argument: "seeing then that these things cannot be spoken against" — Gill notes the officer intimates that the city's devotion to Diana was so certain and well known that nobody would contradict it; the worship secure, undisputed; the alarm groundless. The expositors observe the conclusion: "ye ought to be quiet, and to do nothing rashly" — since the worship of Diana was undisputed and secure, there was no danger, no cause for the tumult; the mob should be quiet and act not rashly; the call to calm and deliberation. The witnesses press the removal of the mob's fear: the town clerk removes the ground of their alarm — their worship was not in real danger (the city's devotion undisputed); therefore the tumult was needless; the fear removed, the reason for the riot dissolved; the prudent calming. The expositors draw the lesson on the warning against rash action: "do nothing rashly" — the town clerk warns against rashness, against the hasty violence of the mob; the call to deliberation, to reason, to lawful means (v.38-39); the prudent restraint of the passionate; the warning against rash, mob action. The witnesses observe the wisdom of the official: he removes the mob's fear, then warns against rashness; the prudent leading from passion to reason; the wise handling of the inflamed multitude; the demagogue's frenzy answered by the official's calm reason. The expositors note the contrast with Demetrius: Demetrius stirred the mob with fear and passion (v.27-28); the town clerk calms them with reason and assurance; the inflaming and the quieting; the difference between the demagogue and the prudent official. The witnesses draw the lesson on calm and deliberation: the believer marks the town clerk's call to be quiet, the removal of the mob's fear, and the warning against rash action. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the call to calm, the warning against rashness, and the prudent leading from passion to reason. The verse thus records the town clerk's call to calm — be quiet, do nothing rashly — teaching the prudent removal of the mob's fear and the warning against rash action.
37:
"For ye have brought hither these men, which are neither robbers of churches, nor yet blasphemers of your goddess." The witnesses note the town clerk's vindication of the apostles' companions. Barnes explains the Greek (hierosylous) means not "robbers of churches" (no Christian churches yet built) but those who commit sacrilege, plundering temples of their sacred things; the men had not robbed the temple nor blasphemed Diana. The witnesses note the town clerk testifying that Gaius and Aristarchus were neither temple-robbers nor blasphemers of Diana — a heathen official's vindication of the apostles' companions; the innocence of the Christians acknowledged even by their judge. The witnesses note the vindication. The witnesses note the apostles' innocence.

The witnesses note the town clerk vindicating the apostles' companions — neither temple-robbers nor blasphemers; the Christians' innocence acknowledged.

So the witnesses make the verse the town clerk's vindication of the companions. The lesson the expositors draw is the innocence of the Christians, acknowledged even by a heathen official. The witnesses note the testimony: "ye have brought hither these men, which are neither robbers of churches, nor yet blasphemers of your goddess" — the town clerk testifies that the men (Gaius and Aristarchus) had committed no crime; Barnes explains the Greek means not "robbers of churches" (no Christian churches yet built) but those who commit sacrilege, plundering temples; the men had neither robbed the temple of Diana nor blasphemed the goddess. The expositors observe the significance of this vindication: a heathen official, in the midst of the riot, testifies to the innocence of the Christians; the apostles' companions had broken no law, committed no sacrilege, railed at no god; their conduct blameless even in the eyes of their accusers; the Christians' innocence acknowledged. The witnesses press the conduct of the apostles: though Paul taught that idols were no gods (v.26), he and his companions had not robbed the temples nor railed at Diana; their preaching positive (the true God), not abusive (railing at the false); the wisdom and prudence of their conduct; the gospel preached without needless offence. The expositors draw the lesson on the blameless conduct of the Christians: the apostles preached the truth, but did not rob temples or blaspheme idols; their conduct blameless, giving no just occasion for the charge; "having your conversation honest among the Gentiles" (1 Peter 2:12); the Christians' innocence even before their enemies. The witnesses observe the heathen's testimony to the gospel: a pagan official vindicates the Christians; the enemy testifying to their innocence; the conduct of the Christians commending the gospel even to the heathen; the blameless life a witness. The expositors note the wisdom of preaching the truth without needless offence: Paul taught against idolatry, but did not provoke by abusing the idols; the wisdom that preaches the truth positively, without railing; the distinction between confessing the truth and reviling the false; the prudent conduct. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the town clerk's vindication, the innocence of the Christians, and the blameless conduct that commended the gospel. The verse thus records the town clerk's vindication of the companions — neither temple-robbers nor blasphemers — teaching the innocence of the Christians, acknowledged even by a heathen official.
38:
"Wherefore if Demetrius, and the craftsmen which are with him, have a matter against any man, the law is open, and there are deputies: let them implead one another." The witnesses note the town clerk directing them to the lawful courts. Barnes notes "the law is open" — there are court days, appointed for judicial trials; and "deputies" are the Roman proconsuls (Acts 13:7); the cause might be brought before them and decided in a proper manner. The witnesses note the town clerk pointing Demetrius and the craftsmen to the lawful courts — if they had a complaint, let them bring it lawfully before the proconsuls, not by mob violence; the appeal to law against the riot. The witnesses note the appeal to law. The witnesses note the lawful courts.

The witnesses note the town clerk directing them to the lawful courts — if they have a complaint, let them bring it lawfully, not by riot.

So the witnesses make the verse the town clerk directing them to the lawful courts. The lesson the expositors draw is the appeal to law against mob violence, the lawful redress of grievances. The witnesses note the direction: "if Demetrius, and the craftsmen which are with him, have a matter against any man, the law is open, and there are deputies" — the town clerk directs Demetrius and the craftsmen to the lawful courts; Barnes notes "the law is open" means there are court days, appointed for trials; "deputies" are the Roman proconsuls (Acts 13:7); the lawful means of redress available. The expositors observe the appeal to law: "let them implead one another" — if they have a complaint, let them bring it lawfully before the proconsuls, accuse one another in the courts; the lawful redress of grievances; the appeal to law against the riot. The witnesses press the contrast of law and mob: the town clerk directs them from mob violence to lawful process; if they had a grievance, the courts were open; the tumult needless and unlawful; the appeal to law against the disorder of the mob; the lawful way against the riotous. The expositors draw the lesson on the rule of law: grievances are to be redressed by law, not by mob violence; the courts open, the lawful means available; the town clerk upholding the rule of law against the riot; the lawful process against the tumult; "let all things be done decently and in order" (1 Corinthians 14:40). The witnesses observe the wisdom of the official: he does not deny Demetrius a remedy, but directs him to the lawful one; the prudent redirection from violence to law; the wise upholding of order; the official's commitment to lawful process. The expositors note the vindication of the apostles implied: if Demetrius had a real charge, let him bring it to court; the very direction implying that the mob's violence was unjustified, and that no lawful charge had been made; the apostles' innocence (v.37) and the want of lawful accusation; the riot exposed as unlawful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the appeal to law, the lawful courts, and the rule of law against mob violence. The verse thus records the town clerk directing them to the courts — the law is open — teaching the appeal to law against mob violence and the lawful redress of grievances.
39:
"But if ye enquire any thing concerning other matters, it shall be determined in a lawful assembly." The witnesses note the town clerk directing public matters to the lawful assembly. Barnes notes "in a lawful assembly" means an assembly convened, not by tumult and riot, but in conformity to law; this was a tumultuous assemblage, and it was proper for the officer to demand it disperse, public grievances being addressed in a lawful, not a riotous, assembly. The witnesses note the town clerk directing that any public matter be settled in a lawful, regularly convened assembly — not in the riotous, tumultuous gathering; the upholding of lawful order against the mob. The witnesses note the lawful assembly. The witnesses note the upholding of order.

The witnesses note the town clerk directing public matters to a lawful assembly — not the riotous gathering; the upholding of order.

So the witnesses make the verse the town clerk directing public matters to the lawful assembly. The lesson the expositors draw is the upholding of lawful order against the riotous mob. The witnesses note the direction: "if ye enquire any thing concerning other matters, it shall be determined in a lawful assembly" — the town clerk directs that any public matter (other than a private complaint, v.38) be settled in a lawful assembly; Barnes notes "a lawful assembly" means one convened in conformity to law, not by tumult and riot; the public business to be done lawfully. The expositors observe the contrast: the present gathering was a tumultuous, riotous assemblage, not a lawful assembly; the town clerk directs that public matters be addressed in a properly convened, lawful assembly; the disorder of the mob against the order of law; the riot not the place for public business. The witnesses press the upholding of order: the town clerk insists that public matters be settled lawfully, in a regular assembly, not in the riotous mob; the upholding of lawful order against the tumult; the official's commitment to due process; the order of law against the disorder of the riot. The expositors draw the lesson on lawful order: public business is to be done in lawful, orderly assemblies, not by mob; "let all things be done decently and in order" (1 Corinthians 14:40); the town clerk upholding the rule of law; the order against the riot; the lawful way commended. The witnesses observe the prudent restraint: the town clerk, by directing both private complaints (v.38) and public matters (v.39) to lawful means, removes all ground for the riot; the prudent leaving no cause for the tumult; the official's wisdom in dissolving the riot by pointing to lawful remedies; the demagogue's frenzy answered by the appeal to order. The expositors note the implied rebuke of the mob: the present assembly was unlawful, riotous; the town clerk's direction to a "lawful assembly" rebukes the present unlawful one; the riot exposed as disorderly and illegal; the rebuke implied. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the direction to a lawful assembly, the upholding of order, and the rule of law against the mob. The verse thus records the town clerk directing public matters to a lawful assembly — not the riotous gathering — teaching the upholding of lawful order against the riotous mob.
40:
"For we are in danger to be called in question for this day's uproar, there being no cause whereby we may give an account of this concourse." The witnesses note the town clerk's warning of the danger of the riot. Gill notes they were liable to be called to account, reproved, and punished by the Roman proconsul or emperor for this day's uproar, capable of being interpreted as a riot and sedition. Barnes notes such a tumult would be likely to attract the displeasure of the magistrates; popular commotions were justly dreaded by the Roman government. The witnesses note the town clerk warning that the riot endangered the city — liable to be called to account by Rome for the tumult, there being no just cause for it; the fear of Roman displeasure quieting the mob. The witnesses note the danger of the riot. The witnesses note the fear of Rome.

The witnesses note the town clerk warning of the danger — the city liable to Roman displeasure for the uproar, there being no cause.

So the witnesses make the verse the town clerk's warning of the danger of the riot. The lesson the expositors draw is the fear of Roman displeasure, by which the town clerk quieted the mob. The witnesses note the warning: "we are in danger to be called in question for this day's uproar" — the town clerk warns that the city was in danger of being called to account for the riot; Gill notes they were liable to be reproved and punished by the Roman proconsul or emperor for the uproar, capable of being interpreted as a riot and sedition; the danger to the city. The expositors observe the ground of the danger that Barnes marks: such a tumult would attract the displeasure of the magistrates; popular commotions were justly dreaded by the Roman government, which severely punished riots and sedition; the city in danger of Roman penalty for the uproar; the fear of Rome. The witnesses press the want of justification: "there being no cause whereby we may give an account of this concourse" — there was no just cause for the riot; no lawful reason could be given for the tumultuous gathering; the riot indefensible before Rome; the city unable to justify the uproar; the danger the greater for the want of cause. The expositors draw the lesson on the fear that quieted the mob: the town clerk quieted the mob by the fear of Roman displeasure; the dread of the Roman penalty for sedition; the prudent appeal to their self-interest and fear; the riot stilled by the threat of consequences; the fear of Rome the effectual argument. The witnesses observe the providence in the Roman order: the Roman government's strict suppression of riots was, in providence, a protection — the fear of Rome quieting the mob, preserving the apostles and the church; the providence using the civil order to protect the gospel; the Roman peace a shield. The expositors note the wisdom of the appeal: the town clerk, appealing to the danger and the want of cause, removes the mob's zeal by their fear; the prudent leading from passion to self-preservation; the riot stilled by the dread of consequences; the wise quieting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the warning of danger, the fear of Rome, and the providence using the civil order to quiet the mob. The verse thus records the town clerk's warning of the danger — the city liable for the uproar, there being no cause — teaching the fear of Roman displeasure, by which the mob was quieted.
41:
"And when he had thus spoken, he dismissed the assembly." The witnesses note the dismissing of the mob. Clarke draws the lesson on the power of right words: "from the conduct of this prudent, sensible man, we may learn how much influence" wise words have; and notes the word "assembly" (ekklesia, usually translated "church") here signifies an assembly of any kind, good or bad, legal or illegal. Gill notes he dismissed them after his oration and reasonings. The witnesses note the town clerk, having spoken wisely, dismissing the mob — the riot quieted by prudent words; the providence delivering the apostles through the wisdom of a heathen official; the close of the great Ephesian tumult. The witnesses note the dismissing. The witnesses note the power of prudent words.

The witnesses note the mob dismissed by prudent words — the riot quieted, the apostles delivered through a heathen official's wisdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the dismissing of the mob. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of prudent words to quiet the riot, and the providence delivering the apostles. The witnesses note the dismissing: "when he had thus spoken, he dismissed the assembly" — the town clerk, having spoken wisely, dismissed the mob; Gill notes he dismissed them after his oration and reasonings; the riot quieted, the assembly broken up; the tumult ended. The expositors observe Clarke's lesson on the power of right words: from the conduct of this prudent, sensible man, we may learn how much influence wise words have; "how forcible are right words!" (Job 6:25); the riot, which had raged for hours, quieted by the prudent speech of one wise man; the power of prudent, reasoned words to still the passion of the mob. The witnesses press the providence in the deliverance: the apostles and their companions, in great danger from the mob, were delivered through the wisdom of a heathen official; the providence using the town clerk's prudence to quiet the riot and preserve the church; God delivering his servants by an unexpected instrument; the providence in the deliverance. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence that protects the church: God used the prudence of a heathen official, the order of Roman law, the fear of Rome, to quiet the mob and preserve the apostles; the providence overruling the riot, delivering the church; "the Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations" (2 Peter 2:9); the providence in the deliverance. The witnesses observe Clarke's note on "assembly" (ekklesia): the word usually translated "church" here signifies an assembly of any kind, good or bad, legal or illegal; the riotous mob called an "assembly"; the word's general sense; a point of interest in the term. The expositors note the close of the Ephesian ministry: the riot quieted, the apostles preserved; the great Ephesian tumult ended; Paul soon to depart (Acts 20:1); the close of the long and fruitful Ephesian ministry; the providence preserving him to the end. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the dismissing of the mob, the power of prudent words, and the providence delivering the apostles. The verse thus records the dismissing of the mob — the riot quieted by prudent words — teaching the power of right words and the providence delivering the apostles through a heathen official's wisdom.
